metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_74", "source_md": "Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Energy growth sources and future energy-saving pot\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. The analysis framework of the sources for energy increase in the passenger transport sector in China.", "references": ["analysis framework is proposed to consider different characteristics of traffic modes, as shown in Fig. 3. The effects of main driving factors on total passenger transport energy use are analyzed briefly. Furthermore, a specific investigation into three subsectors of passenger transport is performed in turn.", "# Energy growth sources and future energy-saving potentials in passenger transportation sector in China", "In China, energy use from passenger vehicles is increasing rapidly due to urbanization and income growth, but the contributions of transport service structure shift and energy efficiency improvement, especially future energy-saving directions are controversial and unclear because of the differences in the choices of traffic modes, which may greatly affect the analysis results and their interpretations. This study investigates the growth sources of energy use in the passenger transport sector during 1979-2017 and future energy-saving potentials by 2030 by re-examining statistical data. Results show that the transport service increase and transport structure shift led to the increase in energy use by  $665.25\%$  and  $337.22\%$ , but approximately  $14\%$  of the increased energy use was offset by energy efficiency improvements, and its effect is limited in public transportation sector, compared with private passenger vehicle sector. In public passenger transport sector, adjusting the service or volume structure may be a desirable option while in private passenger vehicle sector the policy focus is more on improving fuel efficiency and reducing average annual kilometers traveled per capita. Overall, there is still at least  $28.3\%$  of energy reduction potential by energy efficiency improvement before 2030 compared to 2017 baseline level. Potential evaluation showed that the current potential of transport service structure adjustment based on industrial planning is limited, which highlights the necessity of reform, involving promoting the sharing transportation, railway, subway and buses.", "In the last two decades, the transport sector has become a major source of energy consumption worldwide. In China, transport energy use increased from 58.63 Mtce (million tons of coal equivalent) in 1995 to 421.91 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of nearly  $10\%$ . Energy use from passenger transport dominates total transport energy use, accounting for nearly  $60\%$  in 2017. By 2030, more than 300 million new rural residents are expected to enter the cities in China, which will have a great impact on the passenger transport energy consumption. Passengers are relatively easily guided by policy measures to change their energy-using behaviors in their daily travel, thus leading to low-carbon", "However, one of the key challenges is that the current energy statistics of passenger transportation are fragmented in China. The lack of comprehensive statistic specification causes that the data is scattered into different sectors in energy statistical system. The data does not properly cover metrics of interest for energy consumption analysis of passenger transportation. Part of the data is covered in the Transport, Storage and Post sector and Residential Consumption sector in China Energy Statistical Yearbook [3]. The former only includes the intercity public passenger transport and", "Many studies have investigated energy consumption and emissions increase in transport sector [2,5,6]. The relationship between transport energy use and socio-economic factors has also been intensively discussed using econometric models [7-11]. Various options to save energy and reduce emissions in the transport sector have been evaluated for their reduction potential, from both the technological and behavioral perspectives [1,12,13]. In transport studies, bottom-up methodology and scenario analysis were often used to study future trends, mitigation options, and reduction potential [14,15]. In particular, road transport and passenger transport have always been the hot topics for evaluating energy consumption and emissions [16,17].", "Either from an economy-wide or a sectoral perspective, the driving forces of increased energy consumption have been extensively investigated [18-20]. In the transportation sector, these studies could be summarized from two perspectives, namely, 1) the sectoral perspective, and 2) the vehicle-based perspective. From the sectoral perspective, changes in energy consumption and emission have been analyzed either for the whole transportation sector or its subsectors. Activity factors, namely Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth, population increase, and propensity to travel, were often found to be the main drivers of growing energy consumption [21-24], which is similar to the passenger transport subsector [25-27]. Other studies have focused more on micro sections of transportation. For example, on the vehicle level, the vehicle population, fuel mix, and vehicle fuel efficiency were", "With accelerating urbanization in China, passenger transport energy use has rapidly increased, from 28.78 Mtce in 1997 to 252.47 Mtce in 2017, with an annual average growth rate of  $11.47\%$  (Fig. 1). Energy use could thus be divided into three development stages. From 1978 to 1990, the intercity public passenger transport was the main traveling mode of passengers, as the urban and the private vehicle subsectors were undeveloped. From 1990 to 2002, energy consumption of the private passenger vehicles gradually caught up with that of the intercity subsector. From 2003 to 2017, the private subsector became the major source of the passenger transport energy use. The energy intensity of passenger transport decreased continually due to technological advances, and the energy intensity of the private passenger vehicles decreased rapidly from 143.68 Tce/Mp-km (ton coal equivalent/million passenger-kilometers) in 1997 to 51.38 Tce/Mp-km in 2017, due to the policy of fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. This corresponds to a huge reduction in fuel consumption. Without this reduction in energy intensity, the fuel consumption in the private passenger transport sector would have increased nearly three times greater than it is today.", "(c) Modal energy intensity  
Fig. 7. Energy consumption and passenger volume share of three modes of the urban public passenger transport, 1978-2017. Data sources: China Statistical Yearbook [4]; China Urban Construction Statistical Yearbook [69]; Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64]; Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars, Fuel consumption evaluation methods and targets for passenger cars [48] and the authors' personal calculation.", "The data includes socio-economic activity, transport structure and energy intensity. The socio-economic activity data includes the population, the number of households and the GDP, which are from the China Statistical Yearbook [4]; the transport-related activity data includes the passenger service of the intercity public passenger transport, the passenger volume of the urban public passenger transport and the vehicle population of the private passenger vehicles, which are from 1) Passenger Kilometers of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry, 2) Basic Statistics on Public Transportation in Cities by Region of the Urban, Rural and Regional Development, 3) Possession of Private Vehicles of the Transport, Postal and Telecommunication Services, and Software Industry in the China Statistical Yearbook [4]. The structure data consists of the service structure of the intercity public modes, the volume structure of the urban public modes and the vehicle type structure of the private subsector, which are calculated based on the above transport-related activity data. The intensity data includes the energy intensity of the three subsectors and the average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles, which are from the Yearbook of China Integrated transport [63], the Statistics bulletin of transportation sector development [64], the Railway Statistics [65], and the Fuel consumption limits for passenger cars [48]. The average annual mileage of the private passenger vehicles is from the survey data in the FEEI model [49].", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth and its contribution increased over time, but the contribution decreased during 1996-2000 (Figs. 5b) and 2008-2010 (Fig. 5c) due to the Asian financial crisis and the global financial crisis respectively. The shift of various modes of passenger transport (structure effect) contributed to the energy consumption decrease in the years leading up to 1990 because the passenger service share of civil aviation decreased (Fig. 5c). Since 1990, the structure effect turned from a negative value to a positive value. After 1990, the highway and civil aviation services were promoted vigorously by the State Council and Civil Aviation Administration of China [50,66]. Therefore, adjusting the transport structure in the intercity subsector, such as promoting the transfer form highway and civil aviation to railway, could be an effective way to save energy. The passenger service intensity effect provides an increasingly negative contribution to the increase in energy consumption during 1978-2017. The energy intensity of the railway, waterway, and civil aviation services decreased rapidly due to technological advancements and improved management.", "The GDP per capita effect was the dominant contributor to energy consumption growth due to rapid economic development in China. The most prominent contribution appeared during 1991-1995 and 2006-2010, with an energy consumption increase of  $275\%$  and  $195\%$ , respectively (Fig. 6b). The passenger volume structure effect was another main driver of the energy consumption growth due to the shift in the dominant mode of transport. From the late 1980s to the 1990s, the taxi industry grew rapidly, owing to the policy of open business implemented by the State Council", "The multiplication decomposition results in the three subsectors are shown in Fig. 9. Five influential factors on energy consumption can be observed and are quite similar in their effects in the intercity and urban passenger transport subsectors. The GDP per capita increase was always the dominant contributor to rises in energy consumption, but this effect was larger in the urban subsector (2.713). The passenger volume/service per GDP played a critical role in reducing energy consumption as opposed to energy intensity in the public transportation sector. In contrast, however, the private transport subsector showed that fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers per capita offset the effect of vehicle stock growth per capita together.", "This study contributes to understanding the underlying driving factors of energy growth and exploring energy efficiency improvement opportunities in passenger transport sector in China. First, a comprehensive picture of passenger transport energy consumption trends during 1979-2017 in China is given. Main drivers of energy growth are briefly identified through re-classifying the fragmented data statistics, which, to our knowledge, are unique and original. Second, the effects of the main driving forces of increased energy consumption are investigated firstly by using a hybrid decomposition method. Third, the future energy-saving potentials of different policy pathways until 2020 and 2030 are estimated and compared involving sharing transportation, structure adjustment, energy efficiency improvement, and urban car ownership control.", "The results show that there are diversified contributors to energy consumption depending on the traffic modes utilized. On the whole, GDP growth per capita, motorization level and shifts in service structure mainly contribute to energy consumption increase in passenger transport sector during the period 1979-2017, and they cause more than 10 times increase in energy consumption, but the actual energy consumption increased just by 8.77 times because of the effect of energy efficiency improvement. For the intercity and urban public passenger transport, the GDP growth per capita, the service or volume structure change, and population growth strongly dominate the changes in energy consumption, and the effect of energy efficiency improvement is not very significant on a relative basis. In the private passenger vehicle sector, however, the situation is significantly different. Fuel efficiency improvement, decline of family size and average annual kilometers traveled per capita together offset the positive impacts from motorization level rise and vehicle type structure change. Especially, the fuel efficiency improvement has enormously contributed to energy conservation since 2010 in China.", "Outlook analysis shows that by 2030 energy conservation potential in intercity public passenger transport sector is limited (16.6%), but there are still huge energy-saving potentials in urban public passenger transport sector (54.3%) and private passenger transport sector (34.3%). They could totally lead to 31.6% of energy consumption reduction in passenger transport sector by 2030.", "In order to reduce energy use in passenger transport sector, multiple policy directions deserve attention. In China, the motorization level could be regulated by some mandatory policies, such as the vehicle license lottery in major cities, such as Beijing and Tianjin. In future, it is essential to give priority to public transport and sharing transport, such as the subway, bus, car-sharing, and bike-sharing. Particularly, first, sharing transportation deserves more attention in China because Chinese people are more likely to accept sharing and collective ideas, and there are less regulatory restrictions in China. Second, the energy intensity reduction targets up until 2020 need to be strictly enforced, especially for fuel efficiency standards for private passenger vehicles. Third, low-intensity transport modes, such as railway, waterway, rail transit, and mini vehicles, are strongly recommended to promote the low-carbon transition.", "[1] Waisman HD, Guivarch C, Lecocq F. The transportation sector and low-carbon growth pathways: modelling urban, infrastructure, and spatial determinants of mobility. Clim Pol 2013;13:106-29.  
[2] Gupta M, Bandyopadhyay KR, Singh SK. Measuring effectiveness of carbon tax on Indian road passenger transport: a system dynamics approach. Energy Econ 2019;81:341-54.  
[3] National Bureau of Statistics of the People's Republic of China (NBS). China energy statistical Yearbook 2018. Beijing: China Statistics Press; 2018. https://www.chinayearbooks.com-tags/china-energy-statistical-yearbook.  
[4] National Bureau of Statistics of the People's Republic of China (NBS). China statistical Yearbook 2018. Beijing: China Statistics Press; 2018. 2018, http://www.stats.gov.cn/tjsj/ndsj/.  
[5] Ong HC, Mahlia TMI, Masjuki HH. A review on energy pattern and policy for transportation sector in Malaysia. Renew Sustain Energy Rev 2012;16(1): 532-42.  
[6] Schäfer AW, Yeh S. A holistic analysis of passenger travel energy and greenhouse gas intensities. Nature Sustainability 2020:1-4.

content.title: Fig. 4. Summary of the major control regulations in China since 2013. PP: power plants; IN: industry; TR: transport; DO: domestic sector; AR: agriculture.
content.summary: The table summarizes China's major control regulations for PM2.5 mitigation from 2013 to 2020, highlighting different action plans and targeted emission sectors.
content.insight: The figure illustrates the shift in China's policy framework, gradually encompassing more emission sectors and precursors to achieve substantial PM2.5 reductions nationwide, emphasizing extensive measures in key regions.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_178", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Summary of the major control regulations in China since 2013. PP: power plants; IN: industry; TR: transport; DO: domestic sector; AR: agriculture.", "references": ["Fig. 4 summarizes the major action plans for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control implemented by the Chinese government since 2013. The policy framework shows a clear trend of gradually involving more categories of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  precursors and emission sectors, since traditional control actions for a single pollutant or a single sector were insufficient to deliver substantial reductions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations as required by the Action Plan. One notable case is that the estimated  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions and sulfate concentrations inversed from satellite observations both rebounded slightly during 2010-2011 due to a rapid increase in industrial activities and less stringent controls on the iron and steel industries (Fig. 2), which surpassed the reduction benefits of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  controls on power plants [31]. As a result, a series of stringent control measures have been implemented in the industrial polluting sector, including strengthening industrial emissions standards, phasing out small-scale high-polluting factories, phasing out outdated industrial capacities, and upgrading industrial boilers [32]. Compared with the control measures for", "As Fig. 4 indicates, control actions have been implemented nationwide, with additional efforts in several key regions. Improving air quality and mitigating  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in core megacities (e.g., Beijing and Shanghai) have been attained due to increased attention from the central government and the public. Beijing provides a success story in tackling  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution after two-decade-long efforts. In fact, the implementation of comprehensive air pollution controls in Beijing was initiated as early as the 1990s. During the 2008 Olympic Games, extensive control measures were implemented in Beijing and surrounding provinces to guarantee good air quality [41]. Ambient concentrations of CO,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  continuously decreased, even as the municipal GDP, resident population, vehicle stock, and energy consumption increased significantly from 1998 to 2013. Owing to the success of air pollution controls for the 2008 Olympic Games, the local leaders recognized that ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations resulted from a combination of emissions, meteorological conditions, and"], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Tabular & Textual", "sub_types": ["Table"]}, "key_entities": ["Air Pollution Prevention and Control Action Plan", "Industrial Green Development Plan", "Coal-Fired Power Plants", "Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei", "Three-Year Action Plan", "PM2.5", "SO2"], "concise_summary": "The table summarizes China's major control regulations for PM2.5 mitigation from 2013 to 2020, highlighting different action plans and targeted emission sectors.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates the shift in China's policy framework, gradually encompassing more emission sectors and precursors to achieve substantial PM2.5 reductions nationwide, emphasizing extensive measures in key regions."}

content.title: Fig. 1. Comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District. (a) Xiaoshan District (the red area) is located in the hinterland of the YRD in China. (b) Comprehensive traffic monitoring achieves full coverage over Xiaoshan District. Each dot represents a set of comprehensive traffic monitoring facilities that can recognize license plates, traffic fluxes, vehicle-specific speed, and vehicle categories. The entire road network over Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Such road segments are divided into three road classes: highways (blue lines), arterial roads (pink lines), and residential streets (green lines). Map data © 2022, Gaode Map.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the comprehensive traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan District, highlighting road classes and monitoring facilities on a map.
content.insight: The traffic monitoring network in Xiaoshan enables detailed tracking of vehicle dynamics, crucial for analyzing the impact of traffic on air quality and public health.

content.title: Figure 3. Binned observed (black) and simulated (red) median vertical profiles of CO, MVK, lumped furan species (FURA = furan + 2-methylfuran + 2,5-dimethylfuran), and phenol concentrations from the FIREX-AQ campaign. For phenol, observations using the PTR-MS are in light grey and those from the CIT-CIMS in dark grey. Horizontal bars show the 25th–75th percentile range of measurements in each vertical  0.5km  bin. The number of observations in each bin is shown on the right side of each panel.
content.summary: The figure displays binned observed and simulated vertical profiles of CO, MVK, furan species, and phenol concentrations from the FIREX-AQ campaign in the US, distinguished by observation methods and regions.
content.insight: The figure shows discrepancies between modeled and observed concentrations, particularly for phenol, suggesting model underestimations and the impact of fires on air composition in different US regions.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_32", "source_md": "An improved representation of fire non-methane org.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\An improved representation of fire non-methane org\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Figure 3. Binned observed (black) and simulated (red) median vertical profiles of CO, MVK, lumped furan species (FURA = furan + 2-methylfuran + 2,5-dimethylfuran), and phenol concentrations from the FIREX-AQ campaign. For phenol, observations using the PTR-MS are in light grey and those from the CIT-CIMS in dark grey. Horizontal bars show the 25th–75th percentile range of measurements in each vertical  0.5km  bin. The number of observations in each bin is shown on the right side of each panel.", "references": ["More details are available from Bourgeois et al. (2022). MVK, furan, 2-methylfuran, and 2,5-dimethylfuran were measured using the NCAR trace organic gas analyzer with a time-of-flight MS (TOGA-ToF) (Apel et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2021). The TOGA-ToF measurements are reported with a detection limit of 0.5 ppt and an uncertainty (accuracy and precision) of  $20\\%$ . Phenol was measured using the NOAA PTR time-of-flight MS (PTR-ToF-MS) with an accuracy of  $25\\%$  (Müller et al., 2014; de Gouw and Warneke, 2007) and by the California Institute of Technology chemical ionization mass spectrometer (CIT-CIMS) with an accuracy of  $30\\%$  (Xu et al., 2021). During the western part of the campaign, the phenol measurements by PTR were affected by a contamination issue above  $8\\mathrm{km}$ , so those data have been removed. Generally, the model captures the differing fire influence in the eastern and the western US. For example, in the eastern US, the model captures vertical profiles of CO well (Fig. 3), while in the western US, the model matches the general shape but underestimates the magnitude of the observations and likely the influence of more sporadic fires in the region. Recent papers have also shown that GEOS-Chem struggles to fully capture large wildfires in the western US (e.g., Carter et al., 2021; O'Dell et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2014) in part because the DM estimates may be underestimated (Carter et al., 2020) and because GEOS-Chem and other air quality models with a fairly coarse resolution have trouble resolving sub-grid processes (Eastham and Ja", "The FIREX-AQ measurements can also be used to evaluate some of our model updates. Figure 3 shows that MVK, an example NMOG for which we added fire emissions in the model, follows a similar model performance to CO. We note that more than  $80\\%$  of simulated MVK during FIREX-AQ comes from fires. The FIREX-AQ summed observations of the same three furan species suggest that our new lumped \"furan\" (FURA) tracer with only fire sources performs well in the eastern and western US (Fig. 4). This suggests that the furan EFs for US fires like those sampled are accurately captured in the Andreae (2019) compilation, although they may also be overestimated and are thus compensating for an underestimate in the DM burned in GFED4s. Figure 3 shows that our addition of fire emissions of phenol still underestimates observed concentrations across all altitudes in the western US and at the surface in the eastern US. Phenol observations from the CIT-CIMS (dark grey) are a factor of 3 lower than those from the PTR-MS (light grey). The model underestimates the lower phenol concentration (CIT-CIMS) by a factor of 8 in the eastern US and 15 in the western US. {"id": "img_69", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Heating area and coal consumption (1997-2018).", "references": ["Due to the lower temperatures in Northern China, burning coal for heating had become the most common way in which residents kept warm in winter. Since the 1950s, the Government of China provided winter heating via coal-fired boilers in the north Huai River areas. Northern cities received free unlimited heating from November 15 to March 15. Up until now, heating has not only been an important indicator of social wealth but has also become necessary for the survival of northern people in winter. According to statistical data released by the Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development (MOHURD) of the People's Republic of China, the annual growth rate of the urban centralized heating area reached  $14.04\%$  during 1991-2017 (Fig. 2). At the same time, coal consumption increased under the rising demand for heating in the two decades prior to 2013. The "coal-to-gas/electricity" policy has generally altered the previous energy consumption structure. However, the rural heating energy consumption structure has largely remained unbalanced, among which coal consumption accounts for approximately  $75\%$ . Moreover, the large scattered coal heating demand has caused a series of environmental and social problems. The combustion of coal in boilers is associated with the release of air pollutants, especially  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and total suspended particles (TSP). Winter heating has also reduced people's life expectancy in northern regions of China [27]. To meet the increasing demand for heating as well as to alleviate air pollutant emissions, the National Development and Reform Commission of the People's Republic of China announced its plan for the WCHP project (covering 2017-2021).", "implementing intervention measures have seen substantial air quality improvements [7,8]. For example, the United States of America has replaced coal with natural gas in generating electric power since 1997 and has achieved  $44\%$  and  $40\%$  reduction in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from the 1997 levels [9]. A cleaner heating technologies program in New Zealand has led to the significant decline of PM emissions and concentrations [10]. Such policies aiming to diversify energy structures with clean and renewable energy have been successful in certain countries, which also has important implications for China [11]. In Northern China, the annual consumption of coal used for heating is approximately 400 million metric tons, nearly  $50\%$  of which is separately distributed for use in rural areas. Moreover, the combustion of scattered coal heating has caused severe haze to occur in winter [12]. Chen et al. found that the combustion of scattered coal heating has led to an approximately two-thirds increase in air pollutants during periods when heating is employed (i.e., winter) compared to periods when heating is not employed (i.e., summer) [13]. Compared to coal, clean energies such as natural gas and wind power emit less  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and thus reduce human health risks from exposure to air pollutants [9]. Therefore, switching from scattered coal", "Studies on the effectiveness of energy transition have not produced a consensus among scholars. Lin and Zhu [18] found that fuel switching in "energy saving and emission reduction" (ESER) policy had failed to decrease  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations. Li and Chen [19] reported that a city that underwent coal-to-gas transition had no significant impact on reducing  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, and this was primarily because the city focused on the replacement of industrial coal while ignoring the combustion of scattered coal heating in rural areas. Additionally, although Zhao and Luo [20] reported that a "coal-to-gas" project decreased pollutant emissions from a household sector by substituting civil urban coal consumption, they confirmed that this project had a limited effect on reducing industrial coal consumption. Liu et al. [21] adopted the GAINS model to examine the effect of coal switching strategy in Jing-Jin-Ji region, and estimated that the primary  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $28\%$  and  $11\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. [22] evaluated the household coal-replacement program in North China and concluded that coal-to-electricity policy achieved the goal of a clean winter, while the high-quality coal replacement policy had no improvement in indoor air quality. Since the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (smog) crisis in the winter of 2013, environmental governance in China has shifted attention to scattered coal heating in rural areas, after which a series of policies were implemented and subsequently achieved remarkable results [23]. However, official reports may have overestimated the benefits of the clean heating policy by simply attributing the decrease in air pollutant concentrations to the promotion of clean heating policies while ignoring the interference of other exogenous energy-saving and emission-reduction policies [24]. Additionally, policy impacts will vary in different regions; thus, it is necessary to identify the validity of such policy among regions. Few studies to date have focused on the evaluation of energy transition policies in China. As an important part of China's energy transition process, it must be determined that whether or not the "Winter Clean Heating Pilot" (WCHP) project improves air quality. Answering this question can offer some guidance in China's future environmental governance while also providing a quantitative reference for other coal-based, energy-consuming developing countries. It could also be beneficial to achieve the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement under the background of climate change.", "On the one hand, even though the WCHP project has proven to be an effective mean to control winter-derived heating emissions, the cost of this project should not be ignored. The conversion cost of "coal-to-gas/electricity" is higher and includes a subsequent increase in the cost of gas. Replacing unprocessed coal with clean coal can be used as a transitional method in the ultimate goal of energy transition for regions that experience considerable financial governmental pressure and low residential income. The government should focus on reforming the cost of electricity/gas by updating to more advanced heating technology and devices. On the other hand, since the reduction effect of the WCHP project on regional air quality varies under different development levels and resource distributions, project control should be delegated to regional governments based on local features rather than following the outdated pattern of "one-size-fits-all". In order to minimize the negative impact of clean heating policies on energy poverty and energy consumption inequality, the government should provide subsidies to clean energy more affordable to low-income groups.", "[1] Pope lii CA, Burnett RT, Thun MJ, Calle EE, Krewski D, Ito K, et al. content.title: Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the reduction percentages of major pollutants due to the WCHP project, depicted through changes in central heating areas and coal consumption from 1997 to 2017.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates the effectiveness of the WCHP project in reducing air pollutants, highlighting the reduction in coal dependence due to increased central heating coverage, primarily benefiting air quality in both eastern and western regions.

{"id": "img_70", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.", "references": ["In order to intuitively illustrate the effects of the WCHP project on air pollutants, we separately calculated percentage reduction effects of this policy in the eastern and western regions (Fig. 3). In the eastern region, decreasing percentages of AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO}_2$ were $15.51\%$ , $14.72\%$ , $71.08\%$ , and $31.22\%$ , respectively. In the western region, corresponding decreasing percentages were $25.02\%$ , $22.25\%$ , $65.43\%$ , and $36.49\%$ , respectively. Decreases of most", "Coal used for heating rural households poses a severe threat to ambient air pollution and human health. In 2017, China implemented the Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project, which was designed to convert energy from "coal-to-gas/electricity". However, the impact of the WCHP project and its associated dynamic changes have not yet been well quantified. Based on daily monitored panel data covering 35 pilot cities, we conducted a difference-in-differences (DID) model to identify whether or not the WCHP project is effective in maintaining air quality. Results showed that AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased by $20.4\%$ , $18.59\%$ , $34.1\%$ , and $68.4\%$ , respectively, and, except for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , these pollutant reductions were greater in Western China than in Eastern China. Furthermore, when certain variables were controlled, such as weather, holidays, GDP, the ratio between secondary industries, income levels, and environmental expenditures, these results were also robust. Parallel trends and placebo tests were additionally used to confirm the reliability of the DID model. The implementation of energy transition could gain the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement. Policy recommendations were finally made on rural-scale energy transition, including reforming the cost of electricity/gas through advanced technology and subsidies to account for regional inequality.", "Table 4 provides the pollutant reduction effects under the WCHP project using the DID model. As a weighted-average indicator of primary air pollutants, AQI can objectively represent the degree of air pollution [40]. $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ had the highest percentage increment derived from scattered coal combustion during winter heating periods. $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ were the main components of acid rain. Hence, we selected AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ as explanatory variables. Results showed that AQI significantly increased (42.79) during periods when heating was employed, while the WCHP project decreased AQI by 8.72, offsetting approximately $20.38\%$ of the negative impacts caused by winter heating. Likewise, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ concentrations decreased by $18.59\%$ due to the WCHP project. This finding indicated that the WCHP project has had a significant reduction effect on air pollution during winter heating periods, particularly for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , since $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased significantly by $68.36\%$ . However, the reduction effect on $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ remained far from the percentage released by the official feedback report (i.e., $33.7\%$ ). These results indicated that the net effect was smaller than the", "

Coal-fired power generation and scattered coal combustion in the eastern region led to extremely high $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions, while abundant clean energy sources (i.e., wind power, natural gas, and geothermal power) in the western region decreased the dependence of coal to some extent. Thus, the WCHP project was more effective in the eastern region.", "winter heating increased $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and $\mathrm{CO}$ concentrations by $41.7\%$ , $26.7\%$ , $19.2\%$ , $8.8\%$ , and $24.4\%$ , respectively. Concentrations of AQI, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and $\mathrm{SO}_2$ decreased by $20.4\%$ , $18.59\%$ , $34.1\%$ , and $68.4\%$ , respectively. This indicated that the WCHP project can effectively reduce pollutant emissions, and the robustness test verified the reliability of our findings. Except for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , the reduction effects in Western China were greater than those in Eastern China. Moreover, the WCHP project led to significant improvements in the air quality of the pilot cities. content.title: Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_71", "source_md": "Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.", "references": ["As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.", "where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.", "As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.", "According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \"WHCD\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \"official\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48]."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["AQI", "PM2.5", "SO2", "NO2", "Eastern region", "Western region", "WCHP project"], "concise_summary": "The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas."}

Since the fuel specifications of the tested vehicles were not detected in this study, their influences on emissions were hard to distinguish. However, the fuel quality in the cities of Eastern China has been greatly improved. Shanghai began to supply ultra-low sulfur (China 5) fuels since 2013 (Wu et al., 2017). Fuel quality is not very responsible for the high emission. Fig. 7 shows the comparisons of second by second emission rates of H-emitting and N-emitting Euro 2 and Euro 3 vehicles in this study. Malfunctions of catalytic convertors after high strength use are the main cause of the high emissions. content.title: Fig. 8. Comparisons of measured emission factors in East Yan'an Road tunnel with calculated emission factors based on dynamometer tests and El guidebook. (a) CO; (b) THC; (c)  NO_x ; (d)  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: The figure compares measured emission factors in a tunnel with those calculated from dynamometer tests and the EI guidebook for CO, THC, NO_x, and PM2.5.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that emission factors are generally underestimated in the EI guidebook, highlighting discrepancies between measured and calculated values. This suggests the necessity for updated models that consider non-exhaust emissions and high-emitting vehicles.

content.title: Fig. 12. A comparison of the emission inventories by EMEP (left-hand panels) and STEAM2 (right-hand panels) for the marine regions surrounding the Danish straits in 2009. The upper and lower panels represent the predicted annual emissions for PM and CO, respectively. For EMEP, the transparent blue color indicates emission estimates lower than  7.7kgkm^{-2}  and  77kgkm^{-2} , for PM and CO, respectively. Grid resolution:  50\times 50km  for EMEP and  1.9\times 3.4km  for STEAM2.
content.summary: Comparison of emission inventories for PM and CO by EMEP and STEAM2 in 2009 around the Danish straits, showing higher emissions in STEAM2 with grid resolutions of 50x50 km and 1.9x3.4 km, respectively.
content.insight: The figure highlights that STEAM2 predicts significantly higher emissions than EMEP, especially in congested ship routes and major harbor areas, indicating variations due to resolution and model differences.

content.title: Fig. 3. The proportion of people exposed to  PM_{2.5}  in BTH, PRD, YRD, FWP region and the whole nation at different years (i.e., 2014, 2017, and 2020). The dot vertical lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\mu gm^{-3} ).
content.summary: The figure illustrates the proportion of populations in different regions of China exposed to PM2.5 levels compared to WHO air quality targets for 2014, 2017, and 2020.
content.insight: Significant reduction in populations exposed to high PM2.5 levels, with BTH showing most improvement, yet most still exceeded WHO IT-3 in 2020, indicating ongoing public health challenges.

The dot vertical lines denote the air quality targets of WHO (i.e., IT-1, IT-2, IT-3, IT-4 and AQG of annual mean concentrations of 35, 25, 15, 10, and  5\\mu gm^{-3} ).", "references": ["Fig. 3 shows the population exposed to ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in pollution control priority regions and in the whole nation in specific years at the beginning and end of the two phases of ACP (2014, 2017, and 2020). In 2020, the corresponding proportion decreased to  $40\\%$ . BTH had the most severe  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, even with the most prominent air quality improvement amongst four priority regions. In 2014, almost all populations in BTH lived in areas where  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeded the WHO IT-1 threshold and this population proportion decreased to  $90\\%$  in 2020. The FWP was not considered as a priority region for air pollution control in APPCAP, and pollution status was more serious during APPCAP as a result of crude industrial production and its gully terrain that was not conducive to the diffusion of pollutants. By contrast, the effect of ACP measures was profound in FWP region under APWBSD with a  $10\\%$  reduction in the population exposed to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} > 35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in 2020 compared to 2017. The exposed population in PRD is relatively low compared to other priority regions, with  $24\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2014 and  $100\\%$  meeting the WHO IT-1 in 2020, which also achieved the \"Beautiful China\" vision. However, almost all populations ( $\\sim 98\\%$  in the whole nation) still lived under the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations exceeding the WHO IT-3 in 2020, not to mention the WHO AQG standard."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "BTH", "PRD", "YRD", "FWP", "WHO", "AQG", "IT-1", "IT-2", "IT-3", "IT-4"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the proportion of populations in different regions of China exposed to PM2.5 levels compared to WHO air quality targets for 2014, 2017, and 2020.", "inferred_insight": "Significant reduction in populations exposed to high PM2.5 levels, with BTH showing most improvement, yet most still exceeded WHO IT-3 in 2020, indicating ongoing public health challenges."}

content.title: Fig. 4. Spatial distribution (at  1km  resolution) of the  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), and PRD region (e) in 2020.
content.summary: The figure shows the spatial distribution of PM2.5-attributable premature deaths per 1 km² in mainland China, highlighting regions such as BTH, YRD, FWP, and PRD in 2020.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates the concentration of PM2.5-attributable deaths, emphasizing areas with high population density and industrial activity as significant contributors despite decreasing PM2.5 levels over time.

The number of attributable premature deaths in most areas  $(\\sim 99.5\\%)$  of China is less than four cases per  $1\\mathrm{km}^2$  in 2020 (Fig. 4). Large numbers of premature deaths", "were identified in dense populations and advanced industrial areas in the BTH, YRD, PRD and FWP regions (Fig. 4 and Table 1), despite the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in these regions decreased over time. The high-density premature death values are mostly found in metropolitan cities such as Beijing, Shanghai and Guangzhou. The largest number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths per unit area ( $1\\mathrm{km}$ ) occurred in the FWP region. The  $4.72\\%$  region of PRD had more than four cases of premature death per  $\\mathrm{km}^2$ , which is higher than other three priority control regions ( $1.76\\%$ ,  $2.47\\%$ , and  $0.99\\%$  in BTH, YRD, and FWP, respectively) and the national average ( $0.35\\%$ ), indicating that the high population density also exacerbates  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths.", "The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths was reduced in  $68.38\\%$  areas of mainland China over 2014 to 2020 (Fig. 5). The most effectively controlled region was BTH, where over  $74\\%$  of the area showed a decline in the number premature deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure. The FWP produced the smallest decrease amongst the four priority control regions, with the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths reducing in only  $26.95\\%$  of the area. Regarding the decrease number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, unlike the largest decrease in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration occurred in BTH region, the largest decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death occurred in YRD region, with a decrease number of 0.037 million  $(17.00\\%)$  in 2020 compared to 2014 (Table 1), followed by the PRD (decrease  $16.45\\%$ ), BTH (decrease  $10.51\\%$ ) and FWP (decrease  $8.99\\%$ ) regions.", "108°0'0\"E  \nFig. 5. Spatial distribution of changes in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature death in mainland China (a), BTH (b), YRD (c), FWP (d), PRD region (e) between 2014 and 2020 (where the numbers of premature deaths in 2014 and 2020 are both zero this is represented by a white background).", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\\sim 27.05\\%$  of global ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\\%$  and  $26\\%$  decrease in the number of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "premature death", "mainland China", "2020", "BTH", "YRD", "FWP", "PRD"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows the spatial distribution of PM2.5-attributable premature deaths per 1 km² in mainland China, highlighting regions such as BTH, YRD, FWP, and PRD in 2020.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates the concentration of PM2.5-attributable deaths, emphasizing areas with high population density and industrial activity as significant contributors despite decreasing PM2.5 levels over time."}

content.title: Figure 4. Sector specific implied emissions factors  ton(TJ)^{-1}  for the year 2010. Percentiles are reported in the box plots (10, 25, 50, 75, and  90^{\circ} ) together with implied emission factors for specific regions (EU27, USA, China and India). Note - For the percentiles the following countries are left out. For CO - for the industry sector: Togo, Eritrea, Congo, Côte d'Ivoire, Kenya, Benin; for the residential sector: Maldives; for the transport sector: North Korea, Afghanistan, Laos, Tajikistan, Mongolia. For  SO_2  - for the industry sector: Namibia, Laos, Jamaica. For  NO_x  - for the residential sector: Maldives; for the TRANSPORT sector: Afghanistan, Laos, North Korea, Tajikistan. For NMVOC - for the energy sector: Bhutan; for the industry sector: Togo, Eritrea, Côte d'Ivoire, Congo, Cameroon, Kenya, Benin, Aruba, Antigua, Bahamas, Ethiopia, Sudan, Senegal, Equatorial Guinea, Central African Rep., Sri Lanka, Angola, Mozambique, Zambia, Jamaica; for the residential sector: American Samoa, Gum, Maldives, Tonga; for the transport sector: Afghanistan, Laos, North Korea. For  PM_{10}  - for the industry sector: Togo, Eritrea, Côte d'Ivoire, Congo, Kenya, Benin, for the transport sector: Afghanistan. For  PM_{2.5}  - for the energy sector: Tajikistan, Luxembourg; for the industry sector: Togo and Eritrea; for the transport sector: Afghanistan. For BC - for the energy sector: Nigeria, Malaysia, Belgium, Oman, Finland, Georgia, Vietnam, Canada, Armenia, Tunisia, Jordan, The Netherlands, Trinidad and Tobago, Algeria, Latvia, United Arab Emirates, Brunei, Turkmenistan, Japan, Mozambique, Congo, Qatar, Bahrain, Moldova, Kyrgyzstan, South Korea, Taiwan, Luxembourg, Bhutan, Tajikistan; for the industry: Trinidad and Tobago, Malta; for the transport sector: Afghanistan. For OC - for the energy sector: Tunisia, Jordan, Trinidad and Tobago, Algeria, United Arab Emirates, Brunei, Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Mozambique, Congo, Qatar, Bahrain, Kyrgyzstan, Taiwan, Myanmar, South Korea, Vietnam; for the industry sector: Bahrain, Eritrea; for the residential sector: Greenland, Gibraltar, Faroe Islands, Saint Pierre et Miquelon; for the transport sector: Afghanistan. For  NH_3  - for the agriculture sector: Faroe Islands, Tajikistan, Greenland, Falkland Islands, Kyrgyzstan, South Korea, Brunei, American Samoa, Malaysia, Trinidad and Tobago, Bahamas, Saint Pierre et Miquelon, Sri Lanka, Suriname, Réunion, Thailand, Indonesia, Japan, Barbados, Bhutan, Guyana, Costa Rica.
content.summary: Sector-specific implied emissions factors for 2010 are displayed using box plots, comparing percentiles and specific regions (EU27, USA, China, India).
content.insight: The figure illustrates regional differences in emissions intensity, highlighting higher implied emissions for India and lower factors for the EU27 and USA in energy and industry sectors.

metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_157", "source_md": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Figure 4. Emission distributions among sectors in Asia in 2010.", "references": ["Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows: $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to $\mathrm{SO}_2$ (50% of total), NMVOC (38%), $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ (48%), and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ (38% of total), $\mathrm{NO}_x$ (29%), and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ (34%) emissions.", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows: $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ , $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ , $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ , $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ (non-methane volatile organic compounds), $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "Recent studies (e.g., Zhang et al., 2009; Kurokawa et al., 2013) tend to use the mosaic approach to supplement the Asian emission inventory developments. To support the NASA's INTEX-B (the Intercontinental Chemical Transport Experiment Phase B) mission (van Donkelaar et al., 2008; Adhikary et al., 2010), Zhang et al. (2009) developed a new emission inventory for Asia for the year 2006 as an update and improvement of the TRACE-P inventory (Streets et al., 2003). Compared to the TRACE-P inventory, the INTEX-B inventory improved emission estimates for China by introducing a technology-based methodology and incorporated several local inventories including BC and OC emissions for India from Reddy et al. (2002a, b), a Japan emission inventory from Kannari et al. (2007), and official emission inventories for the Republic of Korea and Taiwan. In the updated version 2.1 of the REAS inventory (Kurokawa et al., 2013), a few regional inventories developed with local knowledge are also incorporated to improve the accuracy (see Sect. 2.2.1 for details).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "
ItemDescription
Domain29 countries and regions in Asia
Countries and regionsChina, Japan, Democratic People's Republic of Korea, Republic of Korea, Mongolia, India, Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand, Vietnam, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Russia (East Siberia, Far East, Ural, West Siberia)
SpeciesSO2, NOx, CO, NMVOC, NH3, PM10, PM2.5, BC, OC, CO2
VOC speciationby chemical mechanisms: CB05, SAPRC-99
Sectorspower, industry, residential, transportation, agriculture
Spatial resolution0.25° × 0.25°
Seasonalitymonthly
Year2008, 2010
Data Accesshttp://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix
", "NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ .", "We used anthropogenic emissions from REAS2 (Kurokawa et al., 2013) to fill the gap where local emission data are not available. REAS2 updated the REAS version 1.1 for both activity data and emission factors by each country and region using global and regional statistics and recent regional specific studies on emissions factors. Improved from its previous version, power plant emissions in REAS2 were estimated by combining information on generation capacity, fuel type, running years, and $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ emissions from the Carbon Monitoring for Action database (CARMA; Wheeler and Ummel, 2008) and the World Electric Power Plants database (WEPP; Platts, 2009). REAS2 extended the domain to include emissions of Central Asia and the Asian part of Russia (referred to as Russia Asia). Readers can refer to Kurokawa et al. (2013) for detailed data sources of activity rates and emission factors assignments for each country and source type. REAS2 is available for the period of 2000-2008. In this work, we updated the REAS2 to the year 2010, following the same approach documented in Kurokawa et al. (2013).", "Figure 5. Emissions distributions by Asian regions in 2010. ![](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2025-12-03/fa4e3fc1-fa8a-45bf-892c-02fa70317f3a/e9cc836680e45800632823741c804c55925434b698d2c3eee92a55777bcba515.jpg) China Other East Asia India Other South Asia Southeast Asia Central Asia Russia Asia", "Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example, $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$ were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$ estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use", "and high penetration of emission control facilities, while high emissions ratios of $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ were found in Russia Asia and Central Asia due to high fraction of coal use and less emission controls. Other East Asia also has the lowest emission ratios of $\mathrm{CO} / \mathrm{CO}_2$ among different regions, owing to a high contribution from industrial and transportation emissions. In contrast, high emissions from small residential combustions led to low combustion efficiencies and high emission ratio over India and Southeast Asia.", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows: $-8.1\%$ for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , $+19.2\%$ for $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , $+3.9\%$ for $\mathrm{CO}$ , $+15.5\%$ for NMVOC, $+1.7\%$ for $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , $-3.4\%$ for $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , $-1.6\%$ for $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , $+5.5\%$ for BC, $+1.8\%$ for OC, and $+19.9\%$ for $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "(b) Figure 8. Emission changes from 2006 to 2010 by Asian regions for $\mathrm{SO}_2$ (a) and CO (b). Left panel: emissions in 2006 and 2010 by region. $Y$ axis represents emissions by region. $X$ axis represents cumulative emission contribution of regions. The dotted and solid lines represent emissions in 2006 and 2010 respectively. Right panel: the shares of emissions by sectors over China and India in 2006 and 2010.", "total primary energy consumption of Asia has increased by $20.6\%$ during the period of 2005 and 2010 (IEA, 2013). During the same period, $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions decreased in China $(-17.2\%)$ , Other East Asia $(-9.5\%)$ , and Central Asia $(-32.8\%)$ due to effective emission control, while they increased in India $(+23.9\%)$ , Other South Asia $(+23.9\%)$ , and Southeast Asia $(+7.8\%)$ due to growth in coal use and absence of desulfurization devices. The decrease in $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions changes in Asian is dominated by changes in China and India. Figure 8a demonstrates the changes in $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions among Asian regions from 2006 to 2010. Wide installation of FGD in China's coal-fired power plants is the main driving factor of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emission changes over Asia. $\mathrm{SO}_2$", "emissions in China's power plants decreased from $17.2\mathrm{Tg}$ in 2006 to $8.2\mathrm{Tg}$ in 2010, contributing to most of the total $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emission reduction over Asia. In contrast, $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions in India increased by $27\%$ during 2006-2010, owing to the dramatic construction of new power plants and the lack of emission control facilities (Garg et al., 2001, 2006). As a consequence, the Indian share of the total Asian $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions increased from $13\%$ in 2006 to $18\%$ in 2010. $\mathrm{NO}_x$ and NMVOCs emissions were increased in all Asian regions except Other East Asia $(-17.3\%)$ for $\mathrm{NO}_x$ and $-13.0\%$ for NMVOCs respectively), indicating lack of effective control measures for those two species over Asia. Increases of $\mathrm{NO}_x$ and NMVOC emissions are mainly driven by growth in in", "dustrial activities and vehicle population. For $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , remarkable emission increases are observed for China (+22.6%), India (+27.8%), Other South Asia (+20.5%), and Southeast Asia (+23.4%) during 2006-2010. For NMVOC, emissions increased by 14.0, 15.0, 12.3, 24.9, 23.6, and 9.3% for China, India, Other South Asia, Southeast Asia, Central Asia, and Russia Asia respectively. Emission changes of other species are relatively small (i.e., within 6%) during 2006-2010. For CO, $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , emission reductions in China were partly offset by increases of emissions in the South and Southeast Asian regions. CO emissions in China decreased by 5% during 2006-2010 (see Fig. 8b), mainly due to improved combustion efficiency, recycling of industrial coal gases, and strengthened vehicle emission standards. The implementation of new vehicle emission standards and retirement of old vehicles has reduced China's transportation CO and NMVOC emissions by 20 and 30%, respectively, during 2006-2010. While in India, Other South Asia, and Southeast Asia, CO emissions increased by 21, 11, and 16%, respectively, between 2006 and 2010.", "Monthly emissions by sector and by Asian region are provided in Tables S6-S14. Monthly profiles in emissions are highly sector dependent given that monthly activity rates vary among different sectors. Figure 10 illustrates the monthly variations of Asian $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emissions by sector for the year 2010. Different species generally show similar monthly emission patterns within the same sector, indicating that monthly emission profiles of each sector are dominated by monthly variations in activity rates. For example, industrial emissions are higher in the second half of the year induced by larger industrial productions to meet the annual total production target. The most significant monthly variation with a winter peak was found in the residential sector, reflecting the higher energy demand for residential heating in winter. Residential $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions in winter are even higher than other species, because $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions from China dominate residential emissions in Asia (70% of total), of which coal consumption in winter is higher than other regions for heating. Monthly profiles of CO emissions are different from other species for the transportation sector. This is because the CO emission factor in winter is higher than in other seasons due to additional emissions from the cold-start process (Kurokawa et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014).", "In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$ resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Russia Asia is not included in the comparison.", "Figure 14b further compares the emission ratios of $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ in the three inventories for individual power plants over Shanxi. Large deviations of $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ ratios in MIX are driven by variations of fuel quality, combustion efficiency, and FGD removal efficiency in each plant, which are precisely represented in CPED. In CPED, there is a tendency towards a decrease in $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ emission ratio with increase of plant size (corresponding to higher $\mathrm{CO}_2$ emissions), in accordance with the legislation that large units were required to be equipped with FGD during 2005-2010 (Zhang et al., 2012). Smaller deviations in $\mathrm{SO}_2 / \mathrm{CO}_2$ emission ratios are found in REAS2, because power plant $\mathrm{SO}_2$ emissions in REAS2 were estimated by using the average FGD penetration rates at provincial level (Kurokawa et al., 2013). The constant ratios for all power plants in EDGAR indicate that (a) the emission factors are not varied within China and (b) the spatial distribution treats all power plants equal, which does not take the variations among power plants into consideration.", "The current MIX inventory also has several limitations. Firstly, it provides emissions with aggregated sectoral information, which may be sufficient for the base case model but insufficient for targeted policy cases. Secondly, the MIX inventory is provided with moderate spatial resolution (i.e., $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ ), which could support global and regional models but is still too coarse for urban models. Finally, yet importantly, gridded emissions are only available for 2008 and 2010 to support base years modeling activities in MICS-Asia and HTAP. For other years, modelers could use available global and regional inventories with more complete year coverage (e.g., EDGAR v4.2, REAS2) or extrapolate the gridded MIX inventory to the neighboring years. Developing a complete time series of gridded emission dataset with the", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. content.title: Figure 5. Emissions distributions by Asian regions in 2010.
content.summary: The figure shows 2010 emissions distribution of various species across Asian regions, highlighting China's dominance in most emissions categories and India's significant contribution.
content.insight: Figure 5 illustrates that China and India are the leading contributors to emissions in Asia, with China contributing over 50% to several species, demonstrating regional emission disparities and source contributions.
