{"id": "img_158", "source_md": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Figure 5. Emissions distributions by Asian regions in 2010.", "references": ["Asian emissions in 2010 for 10 species are listed in Table 3 by country and the shares of 2010 emissions by each subregion are presented in Fig. 5. China is the largest contributor for most species except  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , with more than  $50\%$  contribution for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions. Following China, India is the largest contributor for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions (34% of total) and the second largest contributor for all other species. As shown in Fig. 5, Southeast Asia and Other South Asia contribute more than  $20\%$  to NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ , OC, and CO emissions and around  $10\%$  for other species, representing, in particular, a high contribution from biofuel emissions. Contributions from other Asian regions are less than  $10\%$  for all species.", "Figure 5. Emissions distributions by Asian regions in 2010.  
![](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2025-12-03/fa4e3fc1-fa8a-45bf-892c-02fa70317f3a/e9cc836680e45800632823741c804c55925434b698d2c3eee92a55777bcba515.jpg)  
China Other East Asia India Other South Asia Southeast Asia Central Asia Russia Asia", "Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\mathrm{TgNH}_3$", "$31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ ,  $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ ,  $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ ,  $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Emissions from China and India dominate the emissions of Asia for most of the species. We also estimated Asian emissions in 2006 using the same methodology of MIX. The relative change rates of Asian emissions for the period of 2006-2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Model-ready speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were developed following a profile-assignment approach. Monthly gridded emissions at a spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$  are developed and can be accessed from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).", "Recent studies (e.g., Zhang et al., 2009; Kurokawa et al., 2013) tend to use the mosaic approach to supplement the Asian emission inventory developments. To support the NASA's INTEX-B (the Intercontinental Chemical Transport Experiment Phase B) mission (van Donkelaar et al., 2008; Adhikary et al., 2010), Zhang et al. (2009) developed a new emission inventory for Asia for the year 2006 as an update and improvement of the TRACE-P inventory (Streets et al., 2003). Compared to the TRACE-P inventory, the INTEX-B inventory improved emission estimates for China by introducing a technology-based methodology and incorporated several local inventories including BC and OC emissions for India from Reddy et al. (2002a, b), a Japan emission inventory from Kannari et al. (2007), and official emission inventories for the Republic of Korea and Taiwan. In the updated version 2.1 of the REAS inventory (Kurokawa et al., 2013), a few regional inventories developed with local knowledge are also incorporated to improve the accuracy (see Sect. 2.2.1 for details).", "The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated", "This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in", "We used anthropogenic emissions from REAS2 (Kurokawa et al., 2013) to fill the gap where local emission data are not available. REAS2 updated the REAS version 1.1 for both activity data and emission factors by each country and region using global and regional statistics and recent regional specific studies on emissions factors. Improved from its previous version, power plant emissions in REAS2 were estimated by combining information on generation capacity, fuel type, running years, and  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from the Carbon Monitoring for Action database (CARMA; Wheeler and Ummel, 2008) and the World Electric Power Plants database (WEPP; Platts, 2009). REAS2 extended the domain to include emissions of Central Asia and the Asian part of Russia (referred to as Russia Asia). Readers can refer to Kurokawa et al. (2013) for detailed data sources of activity rates and emission factors assignments for each country and source type. REAS2 is available for the period of 2000-2008. In this work, we updated the REAS2 to the year 2010, following the same approach documented in Kurokawa et al. (2013).", "where  $k$  is the region,  $m$  is species type in CB05 or SAPRC-99 mechanisms, and  $n$  is the number of species emitted from source  $i$ . EVOC is the total NMVOC emissions by source type. In this work, emissions in China and other Asian countries were derived from MEIC and REAS2 respectively.  $X(i,j)$  is the mass fraction of species  $j$  in the total NMVOC emissions for source  $i$ , which is taken from the profiles developed by Li et al. (2014). Those profiles were constructed by grouping and averaging multiple profiles from both local measurements and the SPECIATE database (Hsu and Divita, 2009; Simon et al., 2010).  $\mathrm{Mol}(j)$  is the mole weight of species  $j$  and  $C(j,m)$  is the conversion factor between  $j$  and  $m$  obtained from the mapping tables in Carter (2013).", "# 3.1 Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010", "Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.", "Table 4 presents Asian 2010 emissions by region and by sector. Emissions by country and by sector can be downloaded from the MIX website (http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.html). China's anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $28.7\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $29.1\mathrm{TgNO}_x$", "Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "Table 4. Asian emissions by sector in 2010 for each region (units: Tg for  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.", "# 3.2 Changes of Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010", "In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\%$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\%$  for  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\%$  for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\%$  for  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).", "During 2006-2010,  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions increased in China  $(+29.4\%)$ , India  $(+20.4\%)$ , Other South Asia  $(+15.2\%)$ , and Southeast Asia  $(+12.3\%)$  and remained relatively stable for other regions. The increases in  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions are driven by energy consumption growth stimulated by economic development over Asian regions, especially for China and India. As reported by IEA (International Energy Agency), the", "Table 5. Asian emissions in 2006 and trends between 2006 and 2010 (units for emissions: Tg for  ${\mathrm{{CO}}}_{2}$  and Gg for other species)*.", "* numbers in the parentheses represent emission ratios of 2010 to 2006. Bold values are the total emissions and ratios for Asian regions.", "(b)  
Figure 8. Emission changes from 2006 to 2010 by Asian regions for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  (a) and CO (b). Left panel: emissions in 2006 and 2010 by region.  $Y$  axis represents emissions by region.  $X$  axis represents cumulative emission contribution of regions. The dotted and solid lines represent emissions in 2006 and 2010 respectively. Right panel: the shares of emissions by sectors over China and India in 2006 and 2010.", "total primary energy consumption of Asia has increased by  $20.6\%$  during the period of 2005 and 2010 (IEA, 2013). During the same period,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions decreased in China  $(-17.2\%)$ , Other East Asia  $(-9.5\%)$ , and Central Asia  $(-32.8\%)$  due to effective emission control, while they increased in India  $(+23.9\%)$ , Other South Asia  $(+23.9\%)$ , and Southeast Asia  $(+7.8\%)$  due to growth in coal use and absence of desulfurization devices. The decrease in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions changes in Asian is dominated by changes in China and India. Figure 8a demonstrates the changes in  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions among Asian regions from 2006 to 2010. Wide installation of FGD in China's coal-fired power plants is the main driving factor of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission changes over Asia.  $\mathrm{SO}_2$", "emissions in China's power plants decreased from  $17.2\mathrm{Tg}$  in 2006 to  $8.2\mathrm{Tg}$  in 2010, contributing to most of the total  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emission reduction over Asia. In contrast,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions in India increased by  $27\%$  during 2006-2010, owing to the dramatic construction of new power plants and the lack of emission control facilities (Garg et al., 2001, 2006). As a consequence, the Indian share of the total Asian  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions increased from  $13\%$  in 2006 to  $18\%$  in 2010.  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and NMVOCs emissions were increased in all Asian regions except Other East Asia  $(-17.3\%)$  for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and  $-13.0\%$  for NMVOCs respectively), indicating lack of effective control measures for those two species over Asia. Increases of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  and NMVOC emissions are mainly driven by growth in in", "dustrial activities and vehicle population. For  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , remarkable emission increases are observed for China (+22.6%), India (+27.8%), Other South Asia (+20.5%), and Southeast Asia (+23.4%) during 2006-2010. For NMVOC, emissions increased by 14.0, 15.0, 12.3, 24.9, 23.6, and 9.3% for China, India, Other South Asia, Southeast Asia, Central Asia, and Russia Asia respectively. Emission changes of other species are relatively small (i.e., within 6%) during 2006-2010. For CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ , and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , emission reductions in China were partly offset by increases of emissions in the South and Southeast Asian regions. CO emissions in China decreased by 5% during 2006-2010 (see Fig. 8b), mainly due to improved combustion efficiency, recycling of industrial coal gases, and strengthened vehicle emission standards. The implementation of new vehicle emission standards and retirement of old vehicles has reduced China's transportation CO and NMVOC emissions by 20 and 30%, respectively, during 2006-2010. While in India, Other South Asia, and Southeast Asia, CO emissions increased by 21, 11, and 16%, respectively, between 2006 and 2010.", "Figure 9 presents 2010 Asian NMVOC emissions of different chemical groups by region and by sector. Similar to Asian emissions estimated in previous work (Klimont et al., 2002; Li et al., 2014), alkanes and alkenes are the largest contributors to the total Asian NMVOC emissions in 2010 (27 and  $26\%$  of the total respectively), followed by oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs;  $20\%$ ), aromatics  $(17\%)$ , and alkynes  $(7\%)$ . Regionally, shares of alkanes and aromatics are higher in China, Other East Asia, Central Asia, and Russia Asia than other regions, due to large contributions from the industrial sector. Shares of alkynes in Central Asia and Russia Asia are significantly lower than other regions due to a low contribution from biofuel emissions. Sectoral contribution of emissions varies significantly by different chemical groups. Over Asia, the industrial sector is the major source of emissions of alkanes and aromatics. Alkanes emissions from industrial sector are mainly contributed by gas production and distribution  $(19.8\%$  of total industrial emissions), coal combustion  $(17.1\%)$ , and oil", "Figure 9. Speciated NMVOC emissions for the year 2010 by chemical group and by Asian regions. Alkanes: ethane, propane, butanes, pentanes, hexanes, higher alkanes and their isomers. Alkenes: ethane, propene, isoprene, terpenes, higher alkenes and their isomers. Alkynes: ethyne and other alkynes. Aromatics: benzene, toluene, xylene, trimethylbenzene, other aromatics and their isomers. OVOCs: aldehydes (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and higher aldehydes), ketones (acetone and higher ketones), alcohols (methanol, ethanol, and higher alcohols), ethers, and acids. "Others": halogenated hydrocarbons, unidentified species, etc.", "Figure 10. Monthly variations of Asian  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions by sector for the year 2010.  
![](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2025-12-03/fa4e3fc1-fa8a-45bf-892c-02fa70317f3a/9f2ea3261ee32bb0c63e3b3f77bf598158a7f14fca17a9e7de52657445eb7c5a.jpg)  
SO2 CO PM25 CO2", "Monthly emissions by sector and by Asian region are provided in Tables S6-S14. Monthly profiles in emissions are highly sector dependent given that monthly activity rates vary among different sectors. Figure 10 illustrates the monthly variations of Asian  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions by sector for the year 2010. Different species generally show similar monthly emission patterns within the same sector, indicating that monthly emission profiles of each sector are dominated by monthly variations in activity rates. For example, industrial emissions are higher in the second half of the year induced by larger industrial productions to meet the annual total production target. The most significant monthly variation with a winter peak was found in the residential sector, reflecting the higher energy demand for residential heating in winter. Residential  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions in winter are even higher than other species, because  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions from China dominate residential emissions in Asia (70% of total), of which coal consumption in winter is higher than other regions for heating. Monthly profiles of CO emissions are different from other species for the transportation sector. This is because the CO emission factor in winter is higher than in other seasons due to additional emissions from the cold-start process (Kurokawa et al., 2013; Zheng et al., 2014).", "In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \times 0.25^{\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.", "Figure 11. Monthly variations of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions by Asian region for the year 2010.

Other East Asia", "lar given that MIX used emissions estimates in REAS2 for Asian regions except China and India. However, REAS2 has incorporated several recent emission inventories for China (Kurokawa et al., 2013). The differences between REAS and MIX over China and India will be discussed in the following sections.", "though a point-by-point comparison of emission factors between EDGAR v4.2 and MIX is not feasible, we can still speculate that EDGAR v4.2 may overestimate the combustion efficiency and emission control measures in Asia by using an emission factor database from developed countries.  $\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions in EDGAR v4.2 are  $26\%$  lower than in MIX, with a large difference in residential emissions. The differences are mainly from high emission estimates of wastewater treatment sources in REAS2, which were incorporated into MIX for Asian regions except China. MIX estimated  $3.4\mathrm{Tg}\mathrm{NH}_3$  emissions from wastewater treatment in Asia in 2008, which are more than 2 orders of magnitude higher than EDGAR v4.2 estimates. Differences in  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions at the sector level are also large; similar estimates of  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the two datasets are rather a coincidence than real agreements.", "The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).", "Table 9. Uncertainty in emission estimates by Asian regions in 2010 (95 % confidence intervals; unit: %).", "In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\circ} \times 0.25^{\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.", "Adhikary, B., Carmichael, G. content.title: Figure 6. Per capita emissions by sector for 2010 in MIX, ranked by GDP per capita for each country.
content.summary: Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels.
content.insight: The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC.

metadata.keywords: ["per capita emissions", "GDP per capita", "MIX", "emission intensity", "Kuznets curve", "BC", "OC", "CO2", "SO2", "NOx", "CO", "NMVOC", "NH3", "PM2.5", "PM10"]
metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_159", "source_md": "MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Figure 6. Per capita emissions by sector for 2010 in MIX, ranked by GDP per capita for each country.", "references": ["Figure 6 compared per capita emissions by sector and by species in 2010 for each country. Emissions are ranked by GDP per capita of each country. The correlations between emission intensity (per capita emissions) and economic development (GDP per capita) at country level are not always significant because emission intensities are affected by not only economic level but also by other factors such as industrial structure and dominant fuel type. Nevertheless, the changes in emission intensities in general follow the pattern of Kuznets curve for most species except  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ , BC, and OC. Emission intensities tend to increase following the GDP growth first and then tend to decrease for high-income countries. For BC and OC, per capita emissions are higher in developing countries than in developed countries because low-income countries with low incomes tend to use biofuels in which emitted more BC and OC than other fuel types.", "Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\mu \\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\circ} \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.", "Table 4 presents Asian 2010 emissions by region and by sector. Emissions by country and by sector can be downloaded from the MIX website (http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.html). China's anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $28.7\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $29.1\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$", "When using different datasets for different types of aerosols in the same country, additional uncertainties for aerosol emissions might be introduced from the inconsistency in mass balance closure due to differences in spatial proxies (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015), which is the case for Indian emissions in this study. In the MIX inventory, BC and OC emissions were obtained from ANL-India while  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions were taken from REAS2. During the mosaic process of Indian emissions, an additional check was performed by grid for each sector and emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were adjusted to the sum of BC and OC emissions for the grids where the sum of BC and OC emissions exceeds  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["per capita emissions", "GDP per capita", "MIX", "emission intensity", "Kuznets curve", "BC", "OC", "CO2", "SO2", "NOx", "CO", "NMVOC", "NH3", "PM2.5", "PM10"], "concise_summary": "Figure 6 shows 2010 per capita emissions by sector, ranked by GDP per capita for various countries, illustrating sector-specific emission patterns across different economic levels.", "inferred_insight": "The figure indicates varying correlations between economic development and emission intensities, often following a Kuznets curve, showing emissions initially rising then falling with GDP, except for BC and OC."}

content.title: Figure 7. Emission ratios of  SO_2  to  CO_{2} , and CO to  CO_{2}  by Asian regions. CHN is China, OEA is Other East Asia, IND is India, OSA is Other South Asia, SEA is Southeast Asia, CA is Central Asia, and RA is Russia Asia.
content.summary: The figure shows emission ratios of SO2 to CO2 and CO to CO2 across Asian regions, highlighting variations in emission characteristics.
content.insight: The ratios suggest combustion efficiency and emission control levels, with higher SO2/CO2 indicating coal use and low controls, while CO/CO2 shows combustion efficiency discrepancies, particularly low in India's and Southeast Asia's residential sectors.

Grey shaded grids indicate that the comparison is not available due to absence of emission estimates in EDGAR v4.2. Abbreviations of Asian countries and regions are the same as in Fig. 7. Abbreviations of sectors are as follows: POW is power plants; INDU is industry; RES is residential; TRA is transportation; AGR is agriculture; SUM is total. Russia Asia is not included in the comparison.", "

content.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
content.summary: The figure displays PM2.5 emission factors for gasoline and diesel vehicles under different China emission standards (III, IV, V).
content.insight: As emission standards improve, PM2.5 emission factors decrease. Diesel vehicles emit higher PM2.5 levels than gasoline vehicles under the same standard.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_170", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.", "references": ["The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\\%$  of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "$\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.", "Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Microwave digester was used as the pretreatment of collected filter samples. In order to find out the content of crustal elements in the", "The mass concentration of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.", "Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.", "In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\\%$  and  $63.36\\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\\mu \\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\\%$  and  $93.32\\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. content.title: Fig. 4. The OC/EC ratio of carbonaceous compositions in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: The figure shows OC/EC ratios of PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles across various emission standards, highlighting differences in carbonaceous emissions.
content.insight: The OC/EC ratio indicates the source of PM2.5, showing higher organic content in emissions from gasoline vehicles and illustrating how emission standards impact carbonaceous components.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_172", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. The OC/EC ratio of carbonaceous compositions in  PM_{2.5} .", "references": ["The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "# 3.2. Emission characteristics of carbonaceous components in  $PM_{2.5}$", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Scatter Plot"]}, "key_entities": ["OC/EC ratio", "carbonaceous components", "PM2.5", "gasoline vehicles", "diesel vehicles", "China III", "China IV", "China V"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows OC/EC ratios of PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles across various emission standards, highlighting differences in carbonaceous emissions.", "inferred_insight": "The OC/EC ratio indicates the source of PM2.5, showing higher organic content in emissions from gasoline vehicles and illustrating how emission standards impact carbonaceous components."}

content.title: Fig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  PM_{2.5} .
content.summary: Figure 6 illustrates the emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in PM2.5 from gasoline and diesel vehicles under China III, IV, and V standards.
content.insight: The figure shows higher emission factors for Na, Mg, K, and Ca, indicating significant contributions from fuel additives, with lower emissions for other elements from engine wear and catalytic particles.

Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.", "# 3.1. Emission characteristics of inorganic elements in  $PM_{2.5}$", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .", "The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.", "Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.", "The water-soluble ions with the top four emission factors was  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ . The highest emission factors of inorganic elements in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were K, Ca, Na and Mg, while Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and other inorganic elements had lower emission factors.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan was obtained and the largest proportion was OC. content.title: Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.
content.summary: The figure shows the percentages of PM2.5 components—organic carbon, elemental carbon, water-soluble ions, and inorganic elements—emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles.
content.insight: The figure highlights the dominance of organic carbon in PM2.5 emissions and a significant difference in elemental carbon between gasoline and diesel vehicles, important for understanding vehicle pollution.

{"id": "img_174", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.", "references": ["The components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  could be divided into five categories: OC, EC, water-soluble ions, inorganic elements and other components. Each percentages of these five categories in the particulate matter were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 7. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in gasoline vehicles were  $53.93\%$ $13.07\%$ $13.12\%$  and  $0.30\%$  respectively. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in diesel vehicles were  $51.15\%$ $25.17\%$ $14.53\%$  and  $0.44\%$  respectively. Thus, the largest difference in the proportion of various components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles was EC.", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.", "Mukherjee, A., Agrawal, M., 2018. content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China."}

content.title: Fig. 3. (a) Distribution of HC, CO and  NO_{x}  emission factors in the total vehicle fleet. (b) Probability density distribution of HC, CO and  NO_{x}  emission factors, stratified by vehicle emission standards.
content.summary: The figure shows the distribution and probability density of CO, HC, and NOx emission factors in vehicles, differentiated by emission standards in China.
content.insight: The figure highlights the significant heavy-tailed distribution of emissions, indicating that a small percentage of vehicles contribute disproportionately to total emissions. It underscores the importance of managing high emitters.

(b) Probability density distribution of HC, CO and  NO_{x}  emission factors, stratified by vehicle emission standards.", "references": ["Light-duty gasoline vehicles make up the largest share  $(>90\%)$  of all vehicles and are growing the fastest in China (Huang et al., 2017). Thus, enhancing the supervision and management of high emitters is of vital importance. As presented in Fig. 3, our results also show that the distribution curves of CO, HC, and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emission factors have an obvious long tail, indicating that they were mainly concentrated in the low-value part. CO emission factors of  $73.2\%$  vehicles were lower than  $2\mathrm{g / km}$ , with only  $5.4\%$  larger than  $8\mathrm{g / km}$ . At the same time, vehicles with HC emission factors lower than  $0.1\mathrm{g / km}$  made up  $67.8\%$  of the total vehicles. Similarly, around  $62.9\%$  of vehicles had  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emission factors below  $0.1\mathrm{g / km}$ . This highly skewed distribution patterns of CO, HC, and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions were consistent with previous studies (Bishop and Haugen, 2018; Huang et al.,", "Light-duty gasoline vehicles (LDGVs) have made up  $>90\%$  of vehicle fleets in China since 2019, moreover, with a high annual growth rate  $(>10\%)$  since 2017. Hence, accurate estimates of air pollutant emissions of these fast-changing LDGVs are vital for air quality management, human healthcare, and ecological protection. However, this issue is poorly quantified due to insufficient reserves of timely updated LDGV emission factors, which are dependent on real-world activity levels. Here we constructed a big dataset of explicit emission profiles (e.g., emission factors and accumulated milestones) for 159,051 LDGVs based on an official I/M database by matching real-time traffic dynamics via real-world traffic monitoring (e.g., traffic volumes and speeds). Consequently, we provide robust evidence that the emission factors of these LDGVs follow a clear heavy-tailed distribution. The top  $10\%$  emitters contributed  $>60\%$  to the total fleet emissions, while the bottom  $50\%$  contributed  $<10\%$ . Such emission factors were effectively reduced by  $75.7 - 86.2\%$  as official emission standards upgraded gradually (i.e., from China 2 to China 5) within 13 years from 2004 to 2017.", "Based on the newly updated big data of high-resolution vehicle emission profiles, the emission characteristics of light gasoline vehicles were analyzed in this study. The extent to which pollutant emission factors varied with different emission standards, vehicle manufactures and operating modes were illustrated in detail. The significant influence of traffic congestion on vehicle emissions was addressed with the support of the real-time on-road traffic monitoring system in Xiaoshan District. Additionally, several approaches to reducing on-road vehicle emissions under realistic conditions were proposed from a big data perspective. The results showed that the emissions of LDGVs in China have decreased significantly (75.7–86.2 %) with the continuous upgrading of emission standards (i.e., from China 2 to China 5) in the past 13 years. Heavy-tailed distributions were found in CO, HC, and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emission factors. The results showed that the  $10\%$  high emitters contributed  $>60\%$  to the total fleet emissions, significantly higher than previous studies. In order to achieve greater emission reductions in the future, it is necessary to strengthen the gradual replacements of pre-China 3 vehicles, especially those in the  $10\%$  high emitters. Large differences were found in the emissions of vehicles produced by different manufacturers. It was found that the emission factors of the cleanest and most polluted vehicles produced by different manufacturers differed by 33–49 times. Therefore, in the future, the supervision of", "A.L., 2017. content.title: Fig. 4. Hourly average on-road vehicle emission intensities of the  10\%  high emitters under different congestion scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time).
content.summary: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours.
content.insight: High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.
