metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_136", "source_md": "How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\How the Air Clean Plan and carbon mitigation measu\figure_8.jpg", "title": "Fig. 8. Contribution of different factors to the change of  PM_{2.5}  concentrations (a) and  PM_{2.5} -attributable premature deaths (b) in 2014 to 2020 ("Clean production") denotes process control measures, "Optimize fuel structure" denotes structure control measures, "Energy consumption control" denotes source control measures, "Co-effect of C and PM" denotes the co-effect of  CO_{2}  and  PM_{2.5} , "Death rate" denotes health care level).", "references": ["China implemented a stringent Air Clean Plan (ACP) since 2013 to address environmental and health risks caused by ambient fine particulate matter  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . However, the policy effectiveness of ACP and co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures to environment and health are still largely unknown. Using satellite-based  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  products produced in our previous study, concentration-response functions, and the logarithmic mean Divisia index (LMDI) method, we analyzed the spatiotemporal dynamics of premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure, and quantitatively estimated the policy benefits of ACP and carbon mitigation measures. We found the annual  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in China decreased by  $33.65\%$ $(13.41\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  from 2014 to 2020, accompanied by a decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths of 0.23 million  $(95\%$  confidence interval (CI): 0.22-0.27), indicating the huge benefits of China ACP for human health and environment. However, there were still 1.12 million  $(95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by the exposure of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in mainland China in 2020. Among all ACP measures, clean production (contributed  $55.98\%$  and  $51.14\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) and energy consumption control (contributed  $32.58\%$  and  $29.54\%$  to decrease in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) made the largest contribution during the past seven years. Nevertheless, the environmental and health benefits of ACP are not fully synergistic in different regions, and the effectiveness of ACP measures reduced from 2018 to 2020. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  has become one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction since 2018, confirming the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Our study suggests, with the saturation of clean production and source control, more targeted region-specific strategies and synergistic air pollution-carbon mitigation measures are critical to achieving the WHO's Air Quality Guideline target and the UN's Sustainable Development Goal Target in China.", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure-related drivers (i.e., the drivers of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ) avoided 0.10 million net premature deaths in the first phase (2014 to 2017) (Fig. 8b). The measures in ACP fully offset the increase in the number of premature deaths caused by the co-effect of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  as well as the population growth effect, indicating the explicit positive health effect of air quality improvements. The vulnerability factors (i.e., health care level and age structure) contributed to 0.03 million net premature deaths during 2014 to 2017. Amongst them, the improvement in health care level (decrease of baseline death rate) cannot fully offset the net deaths caused by the aging population in China, meaning that the health care level in China needs to be enhanced further. During the APWBSD, improvement in health care became a more effective driver than energy consumption control for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission to avoid  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable deaths, and can, thus, fully offset the effect of population aging. The population aging caused more premature deaths than economic growth, in the phase of APWBSD, which was distinct from APPCAP.", "We provide a comprehensive estimation of the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths, as well as spatiotemporal changes in these, and a quantitative assessment of the effectiveness of ACP methods in mainland China during 2014 to 2020. The two key contributions are: (1) We estimated the spatiotemporal variation in the number of deaths attributable to  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure in mainland China during ACP based on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  product with the finest spatial resolution available to date, and elucidated the spatial discrepancies in policy implementation across China. China's recent clean air actions have achieved great benefits for human health and the environment. However, over  $98\%$  of the population still lived under the risk of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution ( $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations over  $15\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), with 1.12 million ( $95\%$  CI: 0.79-1.56) premature deaths caused by  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution in mainland China in 2020, accounting for  $\sim 27.05\%$  of global ambient  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ -attributable premature deaths. In addition, the environmental and health benefits of China ACP are not fully synergistic in spatial pattern, with the greatest health benefits detected in the YRD region and the best environment benefits identified in the BTH region. To achieve the co-benefits of environment and health, specific policies need to be adopted under different conditions. (2) We quantified the environment and health co-benefits of ACP measures and carbon mitigation from 2014 to 2020. Clean production was the most effective abatement measure in China ACP, reducing net  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and net premature deaths by  $18.35\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and 0.45 million, respectively. The co-effects of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  contributed to a  $23\%$  and  $26\%$  decrease in the number of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths and became one of the major drivers for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and premature deaths reduction in the APWBSD phase. This confirms the clear environment and health co-benefits of carbon mitigation measures. Meanwhile, the effectiveness of process and source control measures reduced during 2018 to 2020. With the exhaustion of end-of-pipe and energy consumption control measures, the future air clean actions should focus more on carbon abatement and energy structure optimization, especially under the background of global climate change mitigation. In addition to mitigation in industrial and energy sectors, it is also critical to explore more cost-effective approaches (e.g., agricultural ammonia mitigation) (Zhou et al., 2021; Gu et al., 2021) for pollution control in other sectors.", "Anenberg, S.C., et al., 2010. content.title: Figure 4. Effects of a drought on  CO_{2}  emissions from thermal power plants in Yunnan Province are presented in panels (a) and (b). The impact of emission control measures on  SO_{2}  emissions during the 7th CISM Military World Games is shown in (c) and (d). (a) Relative changes in  CO_{2}  emissions in Yunnan Province and other provinces from 2019 to 2020. (b) Relative changes in hydropower generation and thermal power generation in Yunnan Province from 2019 to 2020. Since January and February are usually combined in the statistical data released by the National Bureau of Statistics, they are plotted as a whole here. (c) Daily  SO_{2}  emissions before, during, and after the emission control period in Wuhan, other cities in Hubei, and other provinces except Hubei. The light gray shading covers the periods of short-term emission control. The daily emissions are normalized to the value on October 6. (d) Relative changes in average  SO_{2}  emissions during the emission control period compared to the periods before and after this period.
content.summary: The figure depicts the impact of drought on CO2 emissions and power generation types in Yunnan, and SO2 emissions during the CISM Military World Games in Wuhan and surrounding areas.
content.insight: The figure illustrates notable changes in emission patterns due to environmental factors and control measures, highlighting differences in CO2 and SO2 emissions across regions and timeframes.

content.title: Figure 1. Contributions of various types of compounds to the total U.S. emissions mixture. The numbers of compounds of the various types are given in parentheses.

content.insight: The figure underscores the significant contribution of compound types, especially alkanes and benzene rings, to U.S. emissions, providing quantitative insights into their distribution.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_66", "source_md": "Development of a database for chemical mechanism a.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Development of a database for chemical mechanism a\\figure_1.jpg", "title": "Figure 1. Contributions of various types of compounds to the total U.S. emissions mixture. The numbers of compounds of the various types are given in parentheses.", "references": ["The model species assignment database currently includes over 1850 chemical categories that refer either to individual organic compounds or (in about 70 cases) to mixtures of higher-molecular-weight (primarily  $C_{\\geq 12}$ ) isomers that are represented in the same way in all the profile categories and the currently supported mechanisms. For simplicity, all of these are referred to as compounds in the subsequent discussion, to distinguish them from known or unknown mixture categories that are discussed separately below. The numbers of compounds with various chemical structures are shown on Figure 1, which also gives their mole fractions in a total U.S. emissions mixture, discussed below. Approximately half the compounds are hydrocarbons, approximately  $1/3$  are various oxygenates, and approximately  $8\\%$  of the compounds contain nitrogen. Note that there are large numbers of types of compounds besides just alkanes, alkenes, aromatic hydrocarbons, and simple oxygenates.", "precursor (U.S. Code of Federal Regulations, 2014), flags indicating whether the compound is judged to be volatile and whether it is assigned model species, and the model species assignments for the various mechanisms. To aid in making model species assignments, structural information is also given for many of the compounds, using the designations shown on Figure 1.", "The mass fractions of various types of compounds in total U.S. emissions mixture are shown in Figure 1. The mass fractions are generally similar for the other anthropogenic mixtures, as shown in Table S-11 in the SM. The alkanes are the most important for the anthropogenic mixtures, representing  $37 - 46\\%$  of the mass. Alkenes, aromatics, alcohols, and other oxygenates are next in importance but have different", "The development of a database for making model species assignments when preparing total organic gas (TOG) emissions input for atmospheric models is described. This database currently has assignments of model species for 12 different gas-phase chemical mechanisms for over 1700 chemical compounds and covers over 3000 chemical categories used in five different anthropogenic TOG profile databases or output by two different biogenic emissions models. This involved developing a unified chemical classification system, assigning compounds to mixtures, assigning model species for the mechanisms to the compounds, and making assignments for unknown, unassigned, and nonvolatile mass. The comprehensiveness of the assignments, the contributions of various types of speciation categories to current profile and total emissions data, inconsistencies with existing undocumented model species assignments, and remaining speciation issues and areas of needed work are also discussed. The use of the system to prepare input for SMOKE, the Speciation Tool, and for biogenic models is described in the supplementary materials. The database, associated programs and files, and a users manual are available online at http://www.cert.ucr.edu/~carter/emitdb.", "The first step in developing this model species assignment database was to compile a master list of all the chemical categories used in the all the supported databases to specify the chemical compositions of the various profiles. This master list is given in a table in the database along with separate tables giving mappings of chemical category codes used in the various speciation profile databases to the categories in the master list. Although most of the categories in the unified list refer to single organic chemical compounds, a significant number refers to mixtures of varying degrees of complexity and specificity, including many where the compositions could not be determined. The types of categories used and the numbers of categories of each type are listed in Table 3. The approach used in", "The list of profile and total emissions mixtures used to assess the speciation category assignments in this work is given in Table 4. These consist of profile totals (e.g., \"S44 Prof\") where each profile is weighted equally, anthropogenic emissions totals (e.g., \"US Emit\") where the profiles are weighted by the total mass of TOG emissions in various domains assigned to the profiles, and biogenic emissions totals predicted by the BEIS or MEGAN models. The emissions totals are relevant to assessing relative importance in model inputs, whereas the profile totals are useful when assessing contributions of one type of source relative to others, including sources with currently low emissions that may become relatively more important in the future.", "Table 5 summarizes the mass fractions of the various types of chemical categories in the profile and emissions total mixtures. It can be seen that the assignments for the lumped mechanisms are quite comprehensive, with over  $95\\%$  of the mass of anthropogenic emissions, over  $92\\%$  of the mass of the anthropogenic profiles, and all of the mass of the biogenic mixtures assigned model species in the lumped mechanisms. Most of the emissions and profiles are assigned to single-compound categories, but the contribution of mixture categories is non-negligible, ranging from  $9\\%$  to  $15\\%$  of the mass for the anthropogenic mixtures. The mass contributions of compounds assumed to be nonvolatile (which are counted as assigned for the purpose of modeling) are nonzero but relatively small, being  $0.4\\%$  or less for the emissions or profile totals. The contributions of unspecified volatile mixture categories are relatively small, ranging from  $\\sim 2\\%$  for California emissions totals to  $\\sim 8\\%$  for the Asia emissions and SPECIATE 4.4 profiles. The contributions of unassigned compounds, poorly defined categories, and unclassified categories are negligible in all these cases.", "rankings of relative importance in the four anthropogenic emissions mixtures. The relative importance of the other types of compounds is even more variable, especially those with relatively low contributions to the total mass. Alkenes (primarily isoprene and terpenes) are the most important for the biogenic mixtures, but other types of compounds are non-negligible, as shown on Table S-11 in the SM.", "Figure 2 shows that the molar emissions of CB05 PAR, the model species used to represent \"paraffinic\" carbons, are much higher than any other model species emissions in either mechanism. This is because PAR represents carbons in many types of molecules. Figure 3 shows the distribution of types of compounds with carbons represented by PAR in the total U.S. emissions mixture. A significant fraction ( $\\sim 27\\%$ ) of the carbon represented by PAR represents carbons in compounds other than alkanes. Note that the mechanisms for PAR were originally derived based primarily on mechanisms for alkanes that are represented by ALK3 and ALK4 (Gery et al., 1988).", "Figure 3. Mole fractions of carbons in various types of compounds in the U.S. total emissions mixture that are represented by the CB05 PAR model species. Alkanes are classified their representation in the SAPRC-07 condensed mechanism."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Present Core Findings"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["Alkanes", "Benzene rings", "C-C bonds", "Alcohols", "Aldehydes", "Ketones", "U.S. emissions mixture", "Compounds"], "concise_summary": "Figure 1 displays the mass fractions of different compound types contributing to the total U.S. emissions, highlighting the number of compounds in each category.", "inferred_insight": "The figure underscores the significant contribution of compound types, especially alkanes and benzene rings, to U.S. emissions, providing quantitative insights into their distribution."}

{"id": "img_84", "source_md": "Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of biogenic VOC emissions and their cor\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Annual emissions of isoprene, terpene, other VOCs and total BVOCs in 2017.", "supplement": "(a).isoprene;(b).terpene;(c).other VOCs;(d).Totol biogenic VOCs", "references": ["As shown in Fig. 4, BVOC emissions in China are mostly centralized in the northeast and southeast and in southern Yunnan, Hainan and Taiwan Provinces. Specifically, the forest area in Yunnan accounts for nearly  $10\%$  of all forest cover in China. Therefore, Yunnan Province has the highest BVOC emissions, and the large area of tropical rainforest cover in southern Yunnan leads to substantially higher BVOC emissions in the southern region than in other regions. The high emissions in the", "Biogenic volatile organic compounds (BVOCs) emitted from terrestrial ecosystems have substantial effects on the global climate and environmental chemistry. Previous studies have shown that up to  $90\%$  of the total VOC emissions are derived from biogenic sources (Guenther et al. 1995). BVOCs such as isoprene, monoterpenes and sesquiterpenes participate in oxidative chemical reactions in the atmosphere with oxidants such as ozone, OH and  $\mathrm{NO}_3$  radicals. In addition, BVOCs are major sources of secondary organic aerosols (SOA) and new particle formation (Hallquist et al. 2009; Kulmala et al. 2004; Paasonen et al. 2013; Kota et al. 2015). Measurements and modeling of BVOC emissions are of vital importance to understand the carbon cycle, biosphere-atmosphere interactions and climate change (Gu et al. 2017; Hantson et al. 2017).", "Table 3 illustrates the estimated annual emissions of BVOCs in this work and other studies. The estimate of annual BVOC emissions was  $23.54\mathrm{Tg}$ , which is within the range reported in previous estimates, ranging from  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  to  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  between 1990 and 2006. Estimates in the present work are greater than the  $20.6\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2000 estimated by Klinger et al. (2002) and  $12.83\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003 estimated by Chi and Xie (2011). However, the estimates in the present study are lower than the results of Guenther et al. (1995) and Li et al. (2013), who obtained values of  $28.4\mathrm{Tg}$  for 1990 and  $42.5\mathrm{Tg}$  for 2003, respectively. Differences among the discussed studies can be attributed to various factors. It is reported that the forest coverage, the percentage of which increased from  $16.6\%$  in 2003 to  $21.63\%$  in 2014, and the forest area increased from  $1.59\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2003 to  $2.08\times 10^{8}\mathrm{hm}^{2}$  in 2014 (China Forestry Bureau (CFB), 2014), which may be the main reasons for large discrepancies. In addition, BVOC emissions were largely followed by LAI changes (Souri et al. 2017). Chen et al. (2019) utilized satellite data to analyze the annual average MODIS LAI in China from 2000 to 2017 and observed a significant increasing trend in eastern China (the trend was higher than  $18\times 10^{-2}\mathrm{m}^2$  per  $\mathrm{m}^2$  per decade; see Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information). Therefore, the interannual variation of LAI is also an important factor which cause the differences. Besides, some of the discrepancies can also be explained by the use of different algorithms and emission factors. In this study, the MEGAN default global average EFs were applied to estimate BVOC emissions. Li et al. (2012) used a simplified isoprene algorithm (PCEEA) and neglected the effect of soil moisture and detailed canopy information, which resulted in a much lower estimate of  $12.97\mathrm{Tg}$  in 2006 than our study. Yu and Hong (2012) adopted lower EFs and leaf biomass densities for each plant type derived from a small number of local measurements, which caused an underestimation of emissions. There is a large difference of isoprene emission", "northeast are due to the high forest coverage of the genus and eucalyptus forest which have the highest isoprene emission potential. Taiwan's high emissions originate from evergreen broadleaf forests. The distribution of various deciduous, hardwood and mixed broadleaf trees account for the high emissions in southeastern China and the Qinglin Mountains. Owing to the wide coverage of tropical rainforests, the BVOC emissions in Hainan Province are consequently high. Because of the principal contribution of isoprene to total BVOCs, the spatial distribution of total BVOC emissions is similar to that of forest distribution. Terpene emissions are concentrated in the southern part of China (especially in the southeast) because of the high-density coniferous forests there. In southwestern China, especially in the Sichuan-Tibet region, high altitudes and low temperatures on the Tibetan Plateau have led to low levels of emissions in the region. In the Sichuan Basin (SCB), although the forest and shrub coverages are high, isoprene emissions are relatively low. This trend may be caused by the low temperatures in western Sichuan and the low solar radiation in eastern Sichuan, coupled with the closed terrain and extensive cloud cover in the atmosphere Lin and Li, 2016). In addition, high OVOC emissions lead to high total BVOC emissions in most parts of southern China.", "H., Hoffmann, T., Iinuma, Y., Jang, M., Jenkin, M.E., Jimenez, J.L., Kiendler-Scharr, A., Maenhaut, W., McFiggans, G., Mentel, T.F., Monod, A., Prévot, A.S.H., Seinfeld, J.H., Surratt, J.D., Szmigielski, R., Wildt, J., 2009. content.title: Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
content.summary: The figure shows average mixing ratios of VOCs and OFPs in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting methanol, acetaldehyde, and toluene as major contributors.
content.insight: Methanol, acetaldehyde, and toluene significantly contribute to OFP, with methanol's concentration and acetaldehyde's reactivity being prominent factors. Seasonal variations indicate increased biogenic sources in the wet season.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_151", "source_md": "Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.", "references": ["concentration to obtain OFP (ppbv). Fig. 5 shows the average concentration and OFP of each VOC in the dry and wet seasons over six years. The top three species contributing to OFP in the dry and wet seasons were acetaldehyde, methanol and toluene, which accounted for  $83\\%$  and  $78\\%$  of the OFP contributed by all species in the dry and wet seasons, respectively. Methanol made a significant contribution to OFP because its atmospheric concentration was the highest among all VOCs, while acetaldehyde and toluene made significant contributions because of their high reactivity. In addition, the contribution of isoprene to OFP was not negligible and was significantly higher in the wet season than in the dry season, which also reflects the increased contribution of biogenic sources in the wet season. Compared with the study of the VOC emission inventory in the PRD (Zheng et al., 2009; Lyu et al., 2020), the contribution of OVOCs in this study was more significant because the emission inventory study usually evaluates only the primary emission source, while the secondary source of OVOCs, such as acetaldehyde, cannot be ignored. Aldehydes not only have high reactivity but also can generate OH radicals by photolysis (Volkamer et al., 2010; Xue et al., 2016). Therefore, aldehydes are important products and intermediates of photochemistry reactions, and they are also the key VOC species for subsequent research on atmospheric oxidation capacity.", "Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.", "A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\\mathrm{m} / \\mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong", "The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may", "Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  $\\pm \\mathrm{SD}$ ).", "The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\%)$", "PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart", "Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["VOCs", "OFP", "methanol", "acetaldehyde", "toluene", "isoprene", "dry season", "wet season"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows average mixing ratios of VOCs and OFPs in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting methanol, acetaldehyde, and toluene as major contributors.", "inferred_insight": "Methanol, acetaldehyde, and toluene significantly contribute to OFP, with methanol's concentration and acetaldehyde's reactivity being prominent factors. Seasonal variations indicate increased biogenic sources in the wet season."}

content.title: Fig. 2. Overall VOCs characteristics for different emission segments in the two coking plants.
content.summary: Fig. 2 illustrates the VOCs characteristics in different emission segments of two coking plants, highlighting the weight percentage of aromatics, alkanes, alkenes, and alkynes, along with total VOC levels.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates significant differences in VOC emission profiles across coking plant segments. Aromatics are prevalent in SFM, OLM, and BLM, while alkenes dominate SFN, impacting environmental and health risk assessments.

{"id": "img_194", "source_md": "Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. Overall VOCs characteristics for different emission segments in the two coking plants.", "references": ["BLM were  $11.65\mathrm{mgm}^{-3}$ ,  $0.63\mathrm{mgm}^{-3}$ ,  $2.32\mathrm{mgm}^{-3}$  and  $0.27\mathrm{mgm}^{-3}$ , respectively (Fig. 2). The high emission levels of SFN and OLM imply that the non-recovery coking plant and coke oven of the mechanical coking plant pose more serious risks to humans and the ecological environment.", "All monitored VOCs species were categorized into four groups, i.e., alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 2, aromatics were the most abundant compounds of SFM, OLM, and BLM, accounting for  $69.17\%$ ,  $72.57\%$ , and  $45.97\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The VOC patterns which is mainly characterized by aromatics from different emission segments are similar in the same coking plant and that fugitive leakage should not be ignored during the manufacturing process. On the contrary, the proportion of alkenes to total VOCs was  $70.13\%$  for SFN, implying that the overall source profile varies significantly in different plants with different production engineering.", "# Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission Characteristics from Different Types of Coking Plants in China", "The source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated by two typical coking plants, one employing a non-recovery process and the other, a mechanical process, in Shanxi Province, a major coking hub in China, were determined for different segments of coke production during normal operations. The primary components in the stack flue gas were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane for the non-recovery plant and styrene, benzene, and ethylene for the mechanical plant. The fugitive emissions were also monitored at the mechanical plant, and the most abundant species leaking from the oven were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, whereas those leaking from byproducts were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. The stack flue gas at the non-recovery and mechanical coking plants exhibited VOC emission factors of  $96 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively; thus, VOCs released by the former merit greater concern. Since the highest ozone formation potential (OFP) was observed for the stack flue gas at the non-recovery plant ( $80.26 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by fugitive oven emissions at the mechanical plant ( $7.22 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), controlling these VOCs will significantly reduce their conversion into ozone. Overall, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical coking plants will decrease VOC emissions and improve the ambient air quality in China.", "implemented from 2008–2014, it still accounted for 8 million tons of coke in 2017 (CCIA, 2019). Mechanical coking plants produce coke in a carbonization chamber, then recover the byproducts from the coke oven gas, including tar, benzene, and ammonia. Differently, in non-recovery coking plants, the byproducts are burned and the heat is recovered during the coal conversion process (He, 2006). The emission pathways of pollutants vary with different coking process. Pollutants can be discharged into atmosphere from stack, coke oven leaks, or the byproduct production area in a mechanical coking plant. However, for non-recovery coking plants, pollutants are predominantly emitted from the stack; coke oven leaks can be neglected under the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven. Previous studies have found that pollutants such as particulate matter, nitrogen oxide, sulfur dioxide, toxic metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are released into the environment during coke production (Mu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Mu et al., 2014). VOCs emitted from the coking process account for the largest proportion (12.8%) of all VOCs emitted from industrial processes (Wu et al., 2017). Long-term exposure to these pollutants, especially PAHs and VOCs, can lead to adverse health effects in coke plant workers (He et al., 2015).", "The source profiles and emission factors of VOCs are key information for establishing VOCs emission inventories, source apportionment, environmental management, etc. VOCs source profiles and emission factors have been widely researched in the USA, and the results have been integrated into the SPECIATE 4.5 database (U.S. EPA, 2016) and EPA AP-42 (U.S. EPA, 2008), respectively. The emission factors of various sources have also been identified in other countries, such as Denmark (EMEP, 2016) and UK (NAEI, 2018). Considering the large uncertainty inherent in referencing the results from other countries, scientists in China have recently obtained some VOCs' domestic source profiles and emission factors (Shi et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2016; Zhong et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018). For example, for the coking industry, He (2006) reported that C4 and C6 hydrocarbons are the most abundant VOCs in the coking oven stack flue gas from a coke plant, whereas C6 and C8 hydrocarbons are dominant in coal charging and coke discharging. By comprehensively evaluating the coking process and VOCs from the top of the coke oven, Li (2009) revealed that the major VOCs emissions from coke production are benzene, ethane, ethylene, propylene, toluene, propane, and 1-butene. Jia et al. (2009) identified the major VOCs species of coke oven gas as ethylene, ethane, propene, propane, benzene, toluene, and xylene, and showed that the concentration of VOCs from the coal charging process is significantly higher than that from coking. Moreover, Shi et al. (2015) showed that toluene and  $n$ -hexane are the most abundant species in coke production, whereas Li et al. (2018) found that the five most abundant species emitted from the coking process are acetylene, benzene, ethane, toluene, and propene, accounting for  $>86\%$  of total VOCs. The source profiles of VOCs for the coking process differ between different studies. This suggests that VOCs from different emission segments or processes may affect the overall source profile for coke production.", "In terms of VOCs emission factors from Chinese coke production, He (2006) calculated a VOCs emission factor via stack flue gas of  $487.16\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke. Emission factors of VOCs from coal charging  $(56.84\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) and coke discharging  $(1.26\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) have also been reported by Li (2009). In the emission factor guidebooks of U.S. EPA (2008), NAEI (2018), and EMEP (2016), the emission factors of VOCs were given as  $68\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke,  $41\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke, and  $47\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  coal, respectively. This difference between VOC emission factors from different studies could lead to uncertainty in emission estimates. Moreover, previous source profiles only consider the coking process of mechanical coking plants, neglecting coke oven leaks and byproduct production as well as the coking process in non-recovery coking plants; therefore, the existing source profiles may not accurately reflect VOCs emissions from different coking segments. As such, it is crucial to determine localized VOCs source profiles and emission factors based on different emission segments within different types of coking plants in China, specifically, mechanical and nonrecovery coking plants.", "of domestic non-recovery coking plants (MIIT, 2019). A typical non-recovery coking plant and mechanical coking plant in Shanxi are investigated in this study. To evaluate the emission segments from two types of coking plant, samples are collected directly from the stack (non-recovery coking plant and mechanical coking plant) and from emission leaks from the coke oven and byproducts in the mechanical coking plant. The primary objectives of this study are to: (1) establish and compare the VOCs source profiles based on emission segments; (2) calculate the emission factors of VOCs emitted from the stack in different types of coking plants; and (3) investigate the source chemical reactivity of VOCs to access the potential effects of different emission segments on ozone generation.", "Two typical coking plants, a non-recovery coking plant and a mechanical coking plant, were selected to obtain the source profiles of VOCs from different emission segments. The coking plants locate in Shanxi Province, a centralized coking plant area in China. Designed annual yield of coke of non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is 0.5 million tons and 1.6 million tons with the operating load is  $75\%$  and  $75\%$  during the sampling time, respectively. The height of the coke oven in the non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is  $2.1\mathrm{m}$  and  $4.3\mathrm{m}$ , respectively. Detailed information of the coking plants is listed in Table 1. It is important to note the cleaned coal for coking is put into the furnace directly through a closed cleaned coal conveyance corridor in non-recovery coking plant. As the predominantly emitted pathway of pollutants in non-recovery coking plant, stack flue gas is the target in this study. While coke oven leakage is ignored due to the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven during the normal operation. Like most mechanical coking plants, metallurgical coke is produced in coke oven under the positive pressure condition and chemical products, including tar, benzene, and ammonium sulfate, come from coal gas recovery in this studied mechanical coking plant. VOCs can leak from coke oven and pipeline in these processes. Thus, the stack flue gas, oven leaks, and byproduct plant leaks are all investigated in the mechanical coking plant in this study. Samples were collected from the stacks after desulfurization and deducting.", "The overall source profiles were established using the average weight fraction for different coking plant emission segments (SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM) to illustrate the emission characteristics of VOCs in different types of coking plant. A total of 47 VOC species developed by the U.S. EPA PAMS program, which exhibit high reactivity in photochemical reactions, were quantified in this study. The average mass concentration of total VOCs from SFN, SFM, OLM, and", "The source profiles of individual VOC species were also established using the average weight fraction of the four emission segments determined during field sampling, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the most abundant components of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant were ethylene (42.51%), 1-butene (26.27%), benzene (12.11%), acetylene (10.3%), and 2,2-dimethylbutane (2.06%). The sum of C2-C4 alkene hydrocarbons accounted for approximately 69.96% of total VOCs, indicating that the major components released by the non-recovery coking plant were alkene hydrocarbons of low molecular weight (C2-C4). It should be noted that 1-butene and 2,2-dimethylbutane were not appropriate as source indicator species of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant because of their multiple sources. For example, 1-butene is a major emission species of power plants (Yan et al., 2016), solvent use/print ink (Wang et al., 2014), LPG-related sources (Wang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015), and iron and steel facilities (Tsai et al., 2008), whereas 2,2-dimethylbutane is a potential indicator of vehicular exhaust in many regions of the world (Chang et al., 2006). In contrast to stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant, styrene and benzene were the most abundant components of stack flue gas in the mechanical coking plant, accounting for 32.28% and 25.95% of total VOCs, respectively. The next most dominant species with an average weight fraction of 2-15% were ethylene, propane, ethane, m-xylene, toluene, and ethylbenzene. Moreover, high-weight-fraction benzene series were detected in the stack flue gas of the mechanical coking plant. This result was predominantly attributed to the different coking", "As listed in Table S3, COD values between the VOCs profile in SFN and those in previous reported of coking plant (He, 2006; Jia et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2015; U.S. EPA, 2016) analyzed in this study were mostly in the range of 0.5–1, revealing that the profile of SFN differed substantially from the other profiles. However, the VOCs profile of non-recovery coking has either rarely been studied or the few studies have reported very small VOCs emissions from non-recovery coking (U.S. EPA, 2008). The VOCs profile of non-recovery coking plant in this study may be served as a start point for future VOCs emission research in this type coking technology. The COD value was 0.68 between SFN and SFM, which also suggests clear differences in the stack flue gas characteristics of coking plants with different technologies.", "Due to the limited number and coverage of samples in this study, the segment-based VOCs source profile and emission factors will have contained large uncertainties. However, these data are relatively scarce for the coking process in China, especially for the non-recovery coking process. These results will support the existing source profile library and provide insights for assessing domestic VOCs emissions from the coking process, as well as VOCs source apportionment. Future research is required to collect more detailed samples from different segments within the coking process and expand the number of sampled coking plants.", "The OFP was used to assess the chemical reactivity of VOCs from different emission segments, which revealed the priority control emission source according to ozone formation. In this study, the OFP values of SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM were  $80.26\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $1.91\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $7.22\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.21\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. SFN with the higher emission intensity of VOCs ( $11.65\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and consequently to higher ozone formation on the atmosphere should be regarded as a priority emission source. However, the OFP of OLM was considerably greater than that of other sources in the mechanical coking plant, suggesting that control of leakage from the coke oven would substantially reduce the formation of ozone.", "The contribution of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics to OFP from the different segments within coking plants are shown in Fig. 5. Alkenes were the largest contributor to OFP in SFN and aromatics were the largest contributor to OFP in OLM, accounting for  $94.51\%$  and  $70.18\%$  of the total OFP, respectively. These findings are consistent with the concentration characteristics of VOCs. Then, alkenes and aromatics in SFM and BLM exhibited the most abundant contribution to OFP, accounting for  $49.94\%$  and  $45.92\%$ , and  $43.33\%$  and  $40.29\%$  of the total OFP, respectively. A difference was observed between the characteristic contribution to OFP and the concentration characteristics, suggesting that both the concentration and reactivity of VOCs should be considered in order to reduce the ozone formation of VOCs emitted from coking processes.", "For single VOCs species, the top ten largest contributors to OFP from the different emission segments in the coking plants are shown in Fig. S2. Ethylene, propylene, 1-butene, benzene, toluene, xylene, and trimethylbenzene were the largest contributors to the OFP from the four emission segments, indicating that these VOCs are the major pollutants that should be controlled in coking plants.", "He, Q., Yan, Y., Zhang, Y., Wang, X., Wang, Y. img_194.source_reason: 图表揭示出武汉严重的VOC污染水平，建议进一步调查具体的VOCs成分和源头，有助于制定更有针对性的空气质量改善措施。

evaluation.extracted_insight: 图表展示了两家焦化厂中不同排放段的 VOCs 特征，突出各组分中芳烃、烷烃、烯烃和炔烃的权重百分比以及整个 VOCs 水平，显示了在 SFM、OLM 和 BLM 中芳烃的占比显著，而 SFN 中则以烯烃为主。
evaluation.reason: 信息与 VOCs 排放研究相关，但缺乏直接的深挖线索，仅提供背景信息。

content.title: Fig. 3. VOC profiles of (a) stack flue gas (SFN and SFM) and (b) fugitive emissions (OLM and BLM). The ID number of each VOCs species is listed in Table S2.
content.summary: Figure 3 shows the VOC profiles of stack flue gas and fugitive emissions, highlighting major components such as ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and styrene in different emission segments.
content.insight: The figure highlights differences in VOC profiles between non-recovery and mechanical coking plants. Understanding these variations helps to identify and control emissions, with potential to improve air quality by replacing non-recovery plants.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_195", "source_md": "Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C\\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. VOC profiles of (a) stack flue gas (SFN and SFM) and (b) fugitive emissions (OLM and BLM). The ID number of each VOCs species is listed in Table S2.", "references": ["The source profiles of individual VOC species were also established using the average weight fraction of the four emission segments determined during field sampling, and the results are shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the most abundant components of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant were ethylene (42.51%), 1-butene (26.27%), benzene (12.11%), acetylene (10.3%), and 2,2-dimethylbutane (2.06%). The sum of C2-C4 alkene hydrocarbons accounted for approximately 69.96% of total VOCs, indicating that the major components released by the non-recovery coking plant were alkene hydrocarbons of low molecular weight (C2-C4). It should be noted that 1-butene and 2,2-dimethylbutane were not appropriate as source indicator species of the stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant because of their multiple sources. For example, 1-butene is a major emission species of power plants (Yan et al., 2016), solvent use/print ink (Wang et al., 2014), LPG-related sources (Wang et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015), and iron and steel facilities (Tsai et al., 2008), whereas 2,2-dimethylbutane is a potential indicator of vehicular exhaust in many regions of the world (Chang et al., 2006). In contrast to stack flue gas in the non-recovery coking plant, styrene and benzene were the most abundant components of stack flue gas in the mechanical coking plant, accounting for 32.28% and 25.95% of total VOCs, respectively. The next most dominant species with an average weight fraction of 2-15% were ethylene, propane, ethane, m-xylene, toluene, and ethylbenzene. Moreover, high-weight-fraction benzene series were detected in the stack flue gas of the mechanical coking plant. This result was predominantly attributed to the different coking", "Regarding fugitive emissions, the top five species from oven leaks in the mechanical coking plant were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, accounting for  $49.3\\%$ ,  $8.43\\%$ ,  $6.64\\%$ ,  $6.60\\%$ , and  $4.06\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. These results agree with those of previous studies (Li et al., 2018). As presented in Fig. 3(b), the major species of byproduct plant leaks from the mechanical coking plant were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene, accounting for  $34.34\\%$ ,  $9.03\\%$ ,  $7.95\\%$ ,  $7.15\\%$ ,  $5.04\\%$ ,  $4.35\\%$ ,  $4.04\\%$ ,  $2.67\\%$ ,  $2.58\\%$ , and  $2.38\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The sum of C2-C4 hydrocarbons accounted for  $32.04\\%$  of the total VOCs, revealing that C2-C4 hydrocarbons were also the main VOC components emitted from leakage from the byproduct production area in the mechanical coking plant. The VOC characteristic of byproduct plant leaks was different from stack flue gas and oven leaks in the mechanical coking plant because the crude material originates from different coking segments. Stack flue gas and oven leaks in a mechanical coking plant have a similar source for coal gasification process and combustion of coke oven gas, whereas byproduct plant leaks in a mechanical coking plant come from the byproduct recovery process of the coal gas.", "The source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated by two typical coking plants, one employing a non-recovery process and the other, a mechanical process, in Shanxi Province, a major coking hub in China, were determined for different segments of coke production during normal operations. The primary components in the stack flue gas were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane for the non-recovery plant and styrene, benzene, and ethylene for the mechanical plant. The fugitive emissions were also monitored at the mechanical plant, and the most abundant species leaking from the oven were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, whereas those leaking from byproducts were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. The stack flue gas at the non-recovery and mechanical coking plants exhibited VOC emission factors of  $96 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively; thus, VOCs released by the former merit greater concern. Since the highest ozone formation potential (OFP) was observed for the stack flue gas at the non-recovery plant ( $80.26 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by fugitive oven emissions at the mechanical plant ( $7.22 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), controlling these VOCs will significantly reduce their conversion into ozone. Overall, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical coking plants will decrease VOC emissions and improve the ambient air quality in China.", "All monitored VOCs species were categorized into four groups, i.e., alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 2, aromatics were the most abundant compounds of SFM, OLM, and BLM, accounting for  $69.17\\%$ ,  $72.57\\%$ , and  $45.97\\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The VOC patterns which is mainly characterized by aromatics from different emission segments are similar in the same coking plant and that fugitive leakage should not be ignored during the manufacturing process. On the contrary, the proportion of alkenes to total VOCs was  $70.13\\%$  for SFN, implying that the overall source profile varies significantly in different plants with different production engineering.", "As listed in Table S3, COD values between the VOCs profile in SFN and those in previous reported of coking plant (He, 2006; Jia et al., 2009; Shi et al., 2015; U.S. EPA, 2016) analyzed in this study were mostly in the range of 0.5–1, revealing that the profile of SFN differed substantially from the other profiles. However, the VOCs profile of non-recovery coking has either rarely been studied or the few studies have reported very small VOCs emissions from non-recovery coking (U.S. EPA, 2008). The VOCs profile of non-recovery coking plant in this study may be served as a start point for future VOCs emission research in this type coking technology. The COD value was 0.68 between SFN and SFM, which also suggests clear differences in the stack flue gas characteristics of coking plants with different technologies.", "Emission factors are used as essential data for building the emission inventories and for environmental management. Production engineering, equipment, fuel sources, combustion conditions, treatments, etc. are all factors influencing VOCs source emissions. The VOCs EFs for SFN and SFM were  $96\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively (Fig. 4), indicating that the emission intensity from the stack in a non-recovery coking plant is far greater than that in a mechanical coking plant. As the coke oven gas produced by the coking procedure in a non-recovery coking plant operates under negative pressure, the atmospheric pollutants are predominantly discharged through the stack to the atmosphere. However, the EFs of coke production from the mechanical coking plant only involved estimation of the VOCs emissions from the stack flue gas and neglected fugitive emissions such as coke oven and byproduct plant leaks, which partly explains the small EFs from the stack in the mechanical coking plant. As shown in Fig. 4, the EFs of alkenes and aromatics in SFN and SFM were  $67.15\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$  and  $0.29\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively, representing the groups with highest emission intensity. The major species with high emission intensity in SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, and acetylene, with EFs of  $40.58\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $25.26\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $11.71\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $9.89\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. For SFM, the major species were styrene, benzene, and ethylene, with EFs of  $0.16\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $0.08\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $0.04\\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. The results of this study indicate that phasing out non-recovery coking plants in China and strongly promoting mechanical coking plants instead would help reduce VOCs emissions, especially alkene emissions, which is significant for improving the ambient air quality.", "We measured the VOCs emitted from various segments of coke production at a non-recovery plant and a mechanical coking plant in China and then analyzed the source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity. The VOCs were monitored in the stack flue gas of both the non-recovery (SFN) and the mechanical (SFM) plant as well as in fugitive emissions due to leakage", "from the oven (OLM) and from byproducts (BLM) at the mechanical plant. The primary components in the SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane, whereas those in the SFM were styrene, benzene, and ethylene. The OLM was dominated by benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, and the BLM, by benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. These results imply that acetylene and styrene can serve as indicators of non-recovery and mechanical coking processes, respectively. Furthermore, low-molecular-weight hydrocarbons (C2-C4) are synergetic indicators of byproduct emissions at coking plants. The VOCs emission factors for the SFN and SFM were  $96 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \\mathrm{~g} \\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively, demonstrating that VOCs in the stack flue gas of non-recovery coking plants should be controlled due to their high emission intensity. By determining the VOC source profile and emission factor for the SFN, this study provides crucial reference data for the existing source profile library and enables us to assess domestic VOC emissions generated by the coking process. The highest source reactivity was exhibited by the SFN ( $80.26 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the OLM ( $7.22 \\mathrm{mg} \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), which suggests that controlling VOCs in the stack flue gas at non-recovery plants and in the fugitive oven emissions at mechanical plants will greatly reduce the conversion of these pollutants into ozone. content.title: Fig. 4. EFs of major VOCs species and the four major groups emitted from SFN and SFM (22DMC4A: 2,2-dimethylbutane).
content.summary: Figure 4 compares the emission factors (EFs) of major VOCs and their groups from SFN and SFM, highlighting differences in emissions intensity between non-recovery and mechanical coking plants.
content.insight: Non-recovery coking plants exhibit higher emissions of VOCs compared to mechanical coking plants, underscoring the need for improved practices and technologies to reduce air pollution.

content.title: Fig. 5. Contributions of VOC groups emitted from SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM to OFP.
content.summary: The figure shows pie charts illustrating the contribution of VOC groups (alkanes, alkenes, aromatics, alkynes) to OFP from SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM.
content.insight: The figure highlights that alkenes and aromatics are the dominant contributors to OFP in various segments, suggesting a need to focus on their reduction to lower ozone formation in coking processes.

content.title: Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).
content.summary: The figure displays the contribution percentages of various VOC sources to metrics such as MR, L_{OH}, OFP, and SOAP.
content.insight: The dominant sources differ across metrics; combustion sources lead in MR, while industrial sources dominate L_{OH} and SOAP, and vehicle emissions are highest for OFP.

{"id": "img_203", "source_md": "Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).", "references": ["Fig. 6 shows the contributions of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio. The combustion sources contributed most (39.0% or  $23.9 \pm 13.6$  ppbv) to the total VOCs in winter, followed by vehicle emissions (22.5%,  $14.0 \pm 11.3$  ppbv), gasoline evaporation (14.2%,  $8.83 \pm 7.11$  ppbv), industrial sources (12.6%,  $7.92 \pm 10.2$  ppbv), and solvent usage (11.6%,  $6.98 \pm 5.65$  ppbv), respectively.", "As shown in Fig. 6, industrial sources contributed  $32.2\%$  to the total OH loss rate, followed by solvent usage  $(21.4\%)$ , combustion sources  $(20.4\%)$ , vehicle emissions  $(19.4\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(6.61\%)$  respectively. Usually, vehicle emissions or petrochemical industrial emissions dominate man-made VOC reactivity in urban areas. For instance, motor vehicle emissions (dominated by high fractions of aromatics and alkenes) contributed to  $50\%$  of the calculated VOC reactivity in Pasadena (Borbon et al., 2013). Similarly,  $\mathrm{C}_2 \sim \mathrm{C}_6$  alkenes and aromatics from the petrochemical refinery and motor vehicles accounted for  $42\%$  of the total calculated VOC reactivity  $(7.5~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Houston/ Galveston Bay region (Gilman et al., 2009). The percentages of vehicle emissions and industrial sources to OH loss rate in this study were lower than those in the two above researches. However, in this study, the VOC reactivity  $(2.79~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  contributed by vehicle emissions was higher than the traffic-related reactivity  $(1.2~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Northern Front Range Metropolitan Area of Colorado (Abeleira et al., 2017).", "For the OFP of different sources (Fig. 6), vehicle emissions had the largest contribution  $(27.8\%)$ , followed by industrial sources  $(25.4\%)$ , solvent usage  $(20.9\%)$ , combustion sources  $(18.9\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(7.04\%)$ , respectively. The OFP of vehicle emissions in this study was comparable to a previous study in Wuhan that vehicle exhausts accounted for  $23.4 \pm 1.3\%$  of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  formation (Lyu et al., 2016). However, the contribution of industrial sources to OFP was lower than", "Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.", "Alkanes  
Fig. 3. Individual contributions  $(\%)$  of different VOC species (outer circular bar plots) and mean contributions  $(\%)$  (inner donut charts) of alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics to the OH loss rate (a), ozone formation potential (b), and secondary organic aerosol potential (c).", "Fig. 7. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration ranges.", "To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,", "2019). On the contrary, the contributions of combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and solvent usage to OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP decreased with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels. Despite a decreasing trend of combustion sources to the VOC mixing ratio from clean to pollution, it still dominated the VOC origins during the heavy pollution period. Therefore, to alleviate the VOCs pollution, it should prior control combustion sources. From the perspective of VOC reactivity, OFP, and SOAP, reducing VOC emissions from industrial sources would have benefits to both the reduction of VOC levels and SOA during pollution episodes. The results here contributed to better-developing countermeasures of alleviating VOCs, ozone, and particulate matter pollutions.", "The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). For the estimation of OFP, despite the MIR method considers the chemical mechanisms and impacts of the  $\mathrm{VOC} / \mathrm{NO}_x$  ratio on ozone production, it may also introduce simulation uncertainties (Ran et al., 2009).", "the flaws and uncertainty existed, this study also provided an additional approach to understand the contributions of various VOC sources to the reactivity of OH loss rate, ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) using the PMF model.", "Dumanoglu, Y., Kara, M., Altiok, H., Odabasi, M., Elbir, T., Bayram, A., 2014. content.title: Fig. 3. Concentrations in log scales of toxic VOCs in different coating workshops.
content.summary: Fig. 3 illustrates the concentrations of toxic VOCs in various coating workshops, in log scale. Three sectors—container, ship, and furniture—are detailed.
content.insight: The figure highlights significant variations in VOC concentrations across coating sectors, emphasizing the container sector's higher levels due to larger surface areas. It suggests focusing on ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane for health risk reduction.
