metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_216", "source_md": "Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig. 3. Concentrations in log scales of toxic VOCs in different coating workshops.", "references": ["The concentrations of the toxic VOCs selected in this study among different coating workshops are illustrated in Fig. 3 and Table S3. Here, we look details into the species concentrations in different coating workshop in each sector. The most abundant species in primary coating workshop of container sector was toluene  $(3.47 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , followed by ethylbezene  $(2.44 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , m/p-xylene  $(2.14 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , and 1,2-dichloropropane  $(1.13 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ . The secondary coating workshop of container sector had 1-1.5 times higher concentrations of ethylbezene  $(4.7 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , m/p-xylene  $(4.4 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and 1,2-dichloropropane  $(1.70 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , while tertiary coating workshop has even higher concentrations of toluene  $(5.99 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  but much lower concentrations of 1,2-dichloropropane  $(1.41 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ . For the ship coating sector, the species concentrations were much lower than those in container sector. The most prevalent species was ethylbenzene with concentrations of  $8.68 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and  $5.15 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$  in mixing workshop and primary coating workshop, respectively, while was m/p-xylene  $(8.99 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  in secondary coating workshop. In the furniture sector, toluene had the highest concentration in primary coating  $(5.3 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ , followed by secondary coating  $(1.83 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and tertiary coating  $(1.26 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  workshops. From the above observation in different workshops, the concentrations in the same coating sector were generally within the same order of magnitude. However, the difference could be an order of magnitude among different coating sectors. This is mainly because the coating surface areas of container were much larger than the surface areas of ship and furniture components. More solvents were used in the coating processes of container, resulting in larger concentrations of VOCs species.", "Solvent use and paint consumption are significant source sectors of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emissions in China. The occupational painters have high risk of health effect due to exposure to high VOCs concentration. However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. The results show that the container coating had highest cancer risk  $(2.29 \times 10^{-6} - 5.53 \times 10^{-6})$  exceeding the safe limit of  $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ , while non-cancer risk of all workshops was lower than acceptable level of 1. Ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane should be targeted for priority removal during the container coating process in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. This study helps better understand the health risk of VOCs exposure in coating workshops in China and provides information for policy-makers to formulate possible control of specific toxic compounds during coating process.", "Inhalation is the main exposure pathway to the VOCs. Quantitative estimate of carcinogenic risk and non-carcinogenic risk from inhalation exposure to VOCs are developed by different agencies, such as World Health Organization (WHO), US Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA), Office of Environmental Health Hazard Assessment (OEHHA) in California, and Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR) (WHO, 2000; IRIS, 2014; 2017; ATSDR, 2017; OEHHA, 2017). The potency values of the reference concentrations (RfC) for non-cancer risk assessments and inhalation cancer unit risk (UR) for cancer-risk assessments are summarized, which provide a data foundation of quantitative assessment of health risk of VOCs exposures.", "Heath risk assessment of exposure to VOCs have been extensively studied in different environment in China (Du et al., 2014; Huy et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2011; Xiang et al., 2017). Indoor microenvironment received most widely attention from the public because people spend most of the lifetime indoors (Sundell, 2004). Guo et al. (2004) compared the inhalation cancer risk in the home, office, restaurant, and classroom in Hong Kong, and found the smoking home had the highest cancer risk while classroom the lowest. Benzene accounted for over  $40\%$  of the cancer risks in these indoor environments. Zhou et al. (2011) evaluated the personal exposure to VOCs in residential indoor/outdoor, workplace, and vehicle, and estimated the associated cancer risk in Tianjin. It was found that the cancer risk of benzene, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, and 1,3-butadiene exceeded the recommended acceptable limit of 1 per million (US EPA, 2005). Dai et al. (2017) assessed the cancer and non-cancer risk in newly renovated homes Shanghai, China, reporting that halocarbon species such as 1,2-dichloroethane, 1,4-dichlorobenzene, methylenechloride presented the highest cancer risk. In addition to household microenvironment, heath effect assessment of VOCs exposure were studied in industrial workshops. Emission profiles and health risk of VOCs were examined during e-waste dismantling processes and plastic solid waste recycling processes (An et al., 2014; He et al., 2015). Cancer risk and non-cancer risk were then quantified in these manufacturing workshops, with aromatic and halogenated hydrocarbons as the most toxic compounds. Due to a closer proximity with emission sources, VOCs exposure poses a higher threat to the workers in the occupational environment, such as landfill environment (Drumusoglu et al., 2010), printing workshop (Alabdulhadi et al., 2019), wood furniture coating workshop (Tong et al., 2019), petrochemical complex (Mo et al., 2015), coking wastewater treatment plant (Zhang et al., 2019), and asphalt pavement areas (Cui et al., 2020).", "The main purpose of this study is to evaluate the health risk of VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. Field samples were collected in different workshops in three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating sectors. The spectrum of VOC components and their potency values of toxic effect were examined. Non-cancer and cancer risk of toxic species were estimated for the occupational painters in different indoor workshops.", "# 3.2. Toxic VOCs levels in coating workshops", "Fig. 4 and Table S4 compares the total VOCs concentrations, non-cancer risk, and cancer risk in different coating workshops in each sector. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), highest concentration was found in tertiary coating workshop  $(1.56 \times 10^{5} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  of container sector, followed by secondary coating workshop  $(1.54 \times 10^{5} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and primary workshop  $(1.01 \times 10^{5} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  of container sector. They were an order of magnitude higher than those in ship coating sector  $(1.43 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3} - 2.55 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and furniture coating sector  $(9.98 \times 10^{3} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3} - 2.74 \times 10^{4} \mu \mathrm{g} \mathrm{m}^{-3})$ . The major species contributing to the high concentration in container and ship coating sectors were toluene, ethylbenzene, m/p-xylene, o", "Fig. 4 (c) and (f) shows the total cancer risk in different workshops and the contributions from different VOCs species, respectively. The secondary coating workshop of container sector had highest cancer risk  $(5.53 \times 10^{-6})$ , followed by primary coating workshop of container  $(3.40 \times 10^{-6})$ , tertiary coating workshop of container  $(2.29 \times 10^{-6})$ , and mixing workshop of ship sector", "Fig. 4. Comparisons of (a) concentrations, (b) non-cancer risk, and (c) cancer risk in different coating workshops. (d), (e) and (f) are the contributions of different VOC species to the total concentration, total non-cancer risk, and cancer risk.", "Fig. 5. Comparisons of non-cancer risk and cancer risk for individual VOCs in different coating workshops.", "A total of 25 toxic VOCs were selected to assess their non-cancer risk and cancer risk for occupational painters in different coating workshop. The concentration levels varied in different workshops of different sectors. Highest concentration of total VOCs was found in tertiary coating workshop of container sector, followed by secondary coating workshop and primary workshop of container sector. Based on the potency values of non-canner reference concentrations and inhalation cancer unit risk, the non-cancer risk and cancer risk were estimated for the toxic VOCs. Although no noncancer health effect was found, the top five workshops of the relatively high non-cancer risk were identified, including secondary coating workshop of container sector, primary workshop of container, mixing workshop of ship coating sector, tertiary coating workshop of container sector, and primary coating workshop of ship sector. For the cancer risk, the secondary coating workshop of container sector had highest cancer risk, followed by primary coating workshop of container, tertiary coating workshop of container, and mixing workshop of ship sector, which were 1.34-5.53 times of the safe limit of  $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ . The ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane were the major species that contributed to the high cancer risk in these workshops. This study reveals that the container coating had highest cancer risk exceeding the acceptable level. Ship coating had low probability of cancer risk while there was no cancer risk for the furniture coating. Control of ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane during the container coating process should be on priority in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. Because the maximum removing efficiency (99.97%) of the activated carbon-based mask was adopted for the health risk assessment, the non-cancer and cancer risk could be higher if the masks are overused or loss of efficiency of the activated carbon adsorption. Frequent changes of masks are recommended for occupational workers. Last but not the least, it should be noted that the limited number of samples is one of the main limitations in this study. More field samples should be collected in a wider arrays of coating workshops and sectors would help reduce the uncertainty of the risk assessments.", "Alabdulhadi, A., Ramadan, A., Devey, P., Boggess, M., Guest, M., 2019. Inhalation exposure to volatile organic compounds in the printing industry. J. Air Waste Manag. Assoc. 69 (10), 1142-1169.  
An, T., Huang, Y., Li, G., He, Z., Chen, J., Zhang, C., 2014. Pollution profiles and health risk assessment of VOCs emitted during e-waste dismantling processes associated with different dismantling methods. Environ. Int. 73, 186-194.  
Atkinson, R., 2000. Atmospheric chemistry of VOCs and NOx. Atmos. Environ. 34, 2063-2101.

content.title: Fig. 4. Comparisons of (a) concentrations, (b) non-cancer risk, and (c) cancer risk in different coating workshops. (d), (e) and (f) are the contributions of different VOC species to the total concentration, total non-cancer risk, and cancer risk.
content.summary: Fig. 4 presents VOC concentrations, non-cancer risk, and cancer risk across coating workshops, highlighting the contributions of various VOC species to these risks.
content.insight: The figure underscores the elevated cancer and non-cancer risks associated with VOC exposure in specific workshops, with ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane as significant contributors, guiding targeted risk mitigation strategies.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_217", "source_md": "Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Volatile organic compound (VOC) emissions and heal\\figure_4.jpg", "title": "Fig. 4. Comparisons of (a) concentrations, (b) non-cancer risk, and (c) cancer risk in different coating workshops. (d), (e) and (f) are the contributions of different VOC species to the total concentration, total non-cancer risk, and cancer risk.", "references": ["Fig. 4 and Table S4 compares the total VOCs concentrations, non-cancer risk, and cancer risk in different coating workshops in each sector. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), highest concentration was found in tertiary coating workshop  $(1.56 \\times 10^{5} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  of container sector, followed by secondary coating workshop  $(1.54 \\times 10^{5} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and primary workshop  $(1.01 \\times 10^{5} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  of container sector. They were an order of magnitude higher than those in ship coating sector  $(1.43 \\times 10^{4} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3} - 2.55 \\times 10^{4} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  and furniture coating sector  $(9.98 \\times 10^{3} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3} - 2.74 \\times 10^{4} \\mu \\mathrm{g} \\mathrm{m}^{-3})$ . The major species contributing to the high concentration in container and ship coating sectors were toluene, ethylbenzene, m/p-xylene, o", "xylene, and 1,2-dichloropropane, while the main species were toluene, ethylbenzene, and others in furniture coating sector (Fig. 4(d)).", "As shown in Fig. 4(b), the non-cancer risk in all workshops were below 1, indicating that there was no non-cancer health effect to human beings. Nevertheless, the top five workshops with relatively high value of non-cancer risk and their main contributors were identified (Fig. 4b and e). The top five workshops were secondary coating workshop of container sector  $(9.43 \\times 10^{-2})$ , primary workshop of container  $(6.08 \\times 10^{-2})$ , mixing workshop of ship coating sector  $(2.46 \\times 10^{-2})$ , tertiary coating workshop of container sector  $(1.83 \\times 10^{-2})$ , and primary coating workshop of ship sector  $(1.03 \\times 10^{-2})$ . The 1,2-dichloropropane contributed over  $85\\%$  of the total non-cancer risk in container and ship sectors except the tertiary coating workshop of container and secondary coating workshop of ship. The m/p-xylene contributed a lot  $(>30\\%)$  in these two workshops. Unlike the container and ship sectors, m/p-xylene and other VOCs species were main contributors to non-cancer risk in the furniture coating sectors.", "Fig. 4 (c) and (f) shows the total cancer risk in different workshops and the contributions from different VOCs species, respectively. The secondary coating workshop of container sector had highest cancer risk  $(5.53 \\times 10^{-6})$ , followed by primary coating workshop of container  $(3.40 \\times 10^{-6})$ , tertiary coating workshop of container  $(2.29 \\times 10^{-6})$ , and mixing workshop of ship sector", "Heath risk assessment of exposure to VOCs have been extensively studied in different environment in China (Du et al., 2014; Huy et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2011; Xiang et al., 2017). Indoor microenvironment received most widely attention from the public because people spend most of the lifetime indoors (Sundell, 2004). Guo et al. (2004) compared the inhalation cancer risk in the home, office, restaurant, and classroom in Hong Kong, and found the smoking home had the highest cancer risk while classroom the lowest. Benzene accounted for over  $40\\%$  of the cancer risks in these indoor environments. Zhou et al. (2011) evaluated the personal exposure to VOCs in residential indoor/outdoor, workplace, and vehicle, and estimated the associated cancer risk in Tianjin. It was found that the cancer risk of benzene, chloroform, carbon tetrachloride, and 1,3-butadiene exceeded the recommended acceptable limit of 1 per million (US EPA, 2005). Dai et al. (2017) assessed the cancer and non-cancer risk in newly renovated homes Shanghai, China, reporting that halocarbon species such as 1,2-dichloroethane, 1,4-dichlorobenzene, methylenechloride presented the highest cancer risk. In addition to household microenvironment, heath effect assessment of VOCs exposure were studied in industrial workshops. Emission profiles and health risk of VOCs were examined during e-waste dismantling processes and plastic solid waste recycling processes (An et al., 2014; He et al., 2015). Cancer risk and non-cancer risk were then quantified in these manufacturing workshops, with aromatic and halogenated hydrocarbons as the most toxic compounds. Due to a closer proximity with emission sources, VOCs exposure poses a higher threat to the workers in the occupational environment, such as landfill environment (Drumusoglu et al., 2010), printing workshop (Alabdulhadi et al., 2019), wood furniture coating workshop (Tong et al., 2019), petrochemical complex (Mo et al., 2015), coking wastewater treatment plant (Zhang et al., 2019), and asphalt pavement areas (Cui et al., 2020).", "The main purpose of this study is to evaluate the health risk of VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. Field samples were collected in different workshops in three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating sectors. The spectrum of VOC components and their potency values of toxic effect were examined. Non-cancer and cancer risk of toxic species were estimated for the occupational painters in different indoor workshops.", "Fig. 2 and Table S2 shows the average concentration profiles of different coating sectors measured in this study, together with the comparisons with previous studies. The major components were aromatic species (such as toluene, m/p-xylene, and ethylbenzene) and halocarbons species (such as 1,2-dichloropropane) in container, ship and furniture coating. These species were also found to be abundant in furniture coating, automobile coating and metal surface coating in Beijing, Shanghai, and Pearl River Delta (PRD) region (Yuan et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2014; Zheng et al., 2013). Toluene, m/p-xylene, and ethylbenzene together contributed over  $70\\%$  of total VOC emissions. However, variations of the species", "Fig. 5 and Table S5 illustrate the non-cancer and cancer risk of individual species in different workshops. The cancer risk of ethylbenzene  $(1.17\\times 10^{-6} - 2.26\\times 10^{-6})$  was higher than  $1\\times 10^{-6}$  in the three workshops of container coating sector, while the cancer risk of 1,2-dichloropropane  $(2.18\\times 10^{-6} - 3.26\\times 10^{-6})$  was higher than  $1\\times 10^{-6}$  in primary and secondary coating workshop of container sector. Therefore, from the heath effect point of view, the ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane is the priority species which should be removed during the coating processes. Comparisons of non-cancer and cancer risk of key species in different industrial sectors are shown in Table 3. Ethylbenzene was also found to produce high cancer risk in spray paints  $(9.71\\times 10^{-4})$ , wooden furniture manufacturing  $(1.75\\times 10^{-5})$ , municipal solid waste  $(1.71\\times 10^{-6})$ , e-waste dismantling processes  $(6.2\\times 10^{-3})$ , rubber footwear industries  $(>1\\times 10^{-4})$ , petroleum refinery  $(6.09\\times 10^{-3})$  while 1,2-dichloropropane was important in petroleum refinery  $(6.22\\times 10^{-4})$  and solvent use  $(1.47\\times 10^{-2})$ . Benzene and 1,3-butadiene contributed a lot to the cancer risk in petrochemical, rubber footwear, asphalt pavement, coking wastewater treatment", "Fig. 5. Comparisons of non-cancer risk and cancer risk for individual VOCs in different coating workshops.", "A total of 25 toxic VOCs were selected to assess their non-cancer risk and cancer risk for occupational painters in different coating workshop. The concentration levels varied in different workshops of different sectors. Highest concentration of total VOCs was found in tertiary coating workshop of container sector, followed by secondary coating workshop and primary workshop of container sector. Based on the potency values of non-canner reference concentrations and inhalation cancer unit risk, the non-cancer risk and cancer risk were estimated for the toxic VOCs. Although no noncancer health effect was found, the top five workshops of the relatively high non-cancer risk were identified, including secondary coating workshop of container sector, primary workshop of container, mixing workshop of ship coating sector, tertiary coating workshop of container sector, and primary coating workshop of ship sector. For the cancer risk, the secondary coating workshop of container sector had highest cancer risk, followed by primary coating workshop of container, tertiary coating workshop of container, and mixing workshop of ship sector, which were 1.34-5.53 times of the safe limit of  $1.0 \\times 10^{-6}$ . The ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane were the major species that contributed to the high cancer risk in these workshops. This study reveals that the container coating had highest cancer risk exceeding the acceptable level. Ship coating had low probability of cancer risk while there was no cancer risk for the furniture coating. Control of ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane during the container coating process should be on priority in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. Because the maximum removing efficiency (99.97%) of the activated carbon-based mask was adopted for the health risk assessment, the non-cancer and cancer risk could be higher if the masks are overused or loss of efficiency of the activated carbon adsorption. Frequent changes of masks are recommended for occupational workers. Last but not the least, it should be noted that the limited number of samples is one of the main limitations in this study. More field samples should be collected in a wider arrays of coating workshops and sectors would help reduce the uncertainty of the risk assessments."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Compare Experimental Performance", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["VOCs", "toluene", "ethylbenzene", "m/p-xylene", "1,2-dichloropropane", "cancer risk", "non-cancer risk", "workshops"], "concise_summary": "Fig. 4 presents VOC concentrations, non-cancer risk, and cancer risk across coating workshops, highlighting the contributions of various VOC species to these risks.", "inferred_insight": "The figure underscores the elevated cancer and non-cancer risks associated with VOC exposure in specific workshops, with ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane as significant contributors, guiding targeted risk mitigation strategies."}

content.title: Fig. 5. Comparisons of non-cancer risk and cancer risk for individual VOCs in different coating workshops.
content.summary: The figure presents cancer and non-cancer risks of individual VOCs across different coating workshops, highlighting the elevated risks of specific compounds such as ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane.
content.insight: The analysis underscores ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane as priority compounds to remove, to mitigate cancer risks in coating workshops, particularly within container sectors.

However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. In addition to household microenvironment, heath effect assessment of VOCs exposure were studied in industrial workshops. Emission profiles and health risk of VOCs were examined during e-waste dismantling processes and plastic solid waste recycling processes (An et al., 2014; He et al., 2015). Cancer risk and non-cancer risk were then quantified in these manufacturing workshops, with aromatic and halogenated hydrocarbons as the most toxic compounds. Due to a closer proximity with emission sources, VOCs exposure poses a higher threat to the workers in the occupational environment, such as landfill environment (Drumusoglu et al., 2010), printing workshop (Alabdulhadi et al., 2019), wood furniture coating workshop (Tong et al., 2019), petrochemical complex (Mo et al., 2015), coking wastewater treatment plant (Zhang et al., 2019), and asphalt pavement areas (Cui et al., 2020).", "The main purpose of this study is to evaluate the health risk of VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "(d) Slope_NFHWH

content.title: Fig. 2. Trends in national average precipitation pH and total  SO_2  emissions between 1995 and 2017. Precipitation pH data were obtained from 74 sites with continuous observations in the Acid Rain Monitoring Networks [10]; the blue belt indicates the interval within one standard deviation of the mean. Emission data for  SO_2  were derived from the MEIC model.
content.summary: The figure illustrates trends in national average precipitation pH and total SO2 emissions in China from 1995 to 2017, showing a correlation between pH values and emissions.
content.insight: There is a strong negative correlation between SO2 emissions and precipitation pH, with significant policy impact noted during the 11th Five-Year Plan, highlighting past and ongoing efforts to control pollution.

In order to provide accurate estimates of aerosol, reactive gas and greenhouse gas emission fluxes to the atmospheric systems, a global fire assimilation system (GFAS) based on satellite-based fire radiative power (FRP) products is being developed. Equidistant cylindrical projection,  0.5^{\\circ}  resolution. (ECMWF experiment IDs fb15 and fb17)", "references": ["The achieved global sampling is illustrated in Fig. 2, which shows the merged fractions  $\\eta_{j}\\tilde{\\gamma}_{j}$  of satellite observed area of the two MODIS instruments for one 24-h interval. Each instrument performs typically 1-2 effective observations per day of any grid cell. Taking both instruments into account typically yields between 3 and 4 observations per day, from which a single daily emission rate is derived for use in the atmospheric models. We assume with Eq. (9) that the observations provide a sufficiently representative sampling of the diurnal cycle of the fires. With this assumption, the daily mean value of FRP is simply the mean of the FRP  $\\geq 0$  observations. Thus any assumption on the diurnal fire cycle or duration of the observed fires is avoided. The inaccuracy introduced by assuming that the fire observations are representative would clearly be reduced by also taking geostationary fire observations into account, which is planned for future versions of GFAS. In any case, significant observation gaps due to persistent cloud cover remain."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Quantitative Results", "Showcase Prediction Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Geospatial Visualizations", "sub_types": ["Map"]}, "key_entities": ["MODIS", "Terra", "Aqua", "ECMWF", "FRP", "GFAS"], "concise_summary": "The figure illustrates the effective number of satellite observations over grid cells by MODIS instruments on Terra and Aqua on June 4, 2011, highlighting global coverage and observations distribution.", "inferred_insight": "The figure demonstrates that MODIS instruments offer a representative global sampling of grid cells, crucial for deriving daily emission rates for atmospheric models, despite observation gaps from cloud cover."}

img_245.source_reason: 该结果对于理解生物源排放对大气过程的影响是至关重要的，采用深度挖掘策略可探究其更广泛的环境影响，特别是不同季节的LAI对空气质量的作用。

evaluation.extracted_insight: GFED3.1 和 GFASv1.0 数据集中平均碳燃烧分布图比较，GFASv1.0 对低强度火更敏感。
evaluation.reason: 展示了火灾的碳排放分布，与大气模型的火灾事件敏感度有关，适合作为进一步研究火灾低强度排放影响的基础。
